example: Three independent witnesses corroborated the defendant's account of where he had been that evening.
mnemonic: CORROBORATE = ROBUST evidence added (COR-ROBUST-ate) — making the claim stronger.

definition: Familiar with and at ease in many different cultures and countries; broadly inclusive of diverse elements.
example: New York's cosmopolitan population brings together communities from virtually every nation on earth.
mnemonic: COSMOPOLITAN: 'cosmos' (world) + 'polis' (city) — a citizen of the whole world.

definition: To give approval or tolerance to something; to sanction or permit.
example: The administration refused to countenance any form of academic dishonesty.
mnemonic: COUNTER + ANCE: count something as acceptable — give your face (countenance) of approval.
confusableNote: As a noun, countenance means facial expression; as a verb (GRE sense), it means to approve or tolerate.

definition: Made in fraudulent imitation of something genuine; not authentic or real.
example: Customs agents seized thousands of counterfeit handbags bearing forged designer labels.
mnemonic: COUNTERFEIT = COUNTER + FAIT (done) — a counter-done copy, a fake substitute.

definition: Not openly acknowledged or displayed; secret and concealed.
example: The agency ran covert operations that were never disclosed to the public.
mnemonic: COVERT = COVER + t — something covered up and kept from view.
confusableNote: Covert (secret) vs. overt (open and obvious) — direct opposites.

definition: To yearn to possess something belonging to another, especially with envy.
example: He openly coveted his colleague's corner office and prestigious client list.
mnemonic: COVET — hidden in 'cover'; you want to cover what is theirs with your own hands.

definition: Deliberately evasive or reluctant to reveal information, often in a falsely modest or teasing way.
example: When asked about her salary, she was coy, deflecting every question with a smile and a non-answer.
mnemonic: COY — the fish that's hard to catch, always slipping away.

definition: Skilled at achieving goals through clever, often devious, means; artfully cunning.
example: The crafty merchant always managed to sell his inferior goods at premium prices.
mnemonic: CRAFTY = CRAFT (skill) + y — using one's craft slyly to get what one wants.

definition: Contemptibly lacking in courage; shamefully cowardly.
example: The general's craven retreat, abandoning his troops without warning, was deemed treasonous.
mnemonic: CRAVEN — sounds like 'crave in fear': someone who craves only safety, fleeing all danger.

definition: Capable of being believed or trusted; convincingly realistic and worthy of confidence.
example: The witness's calm, consistent testimony made her account the most credible in the trial.
mnemonic: CREDIBLE = CRED — something with credibility you can bank on.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'credulous,' which means too ready to believe things.

definition: A progressive increase in intensity, force, or volume reaching a peak.
example: Public outrage built to a crescendo after the scandal was revealed.
mnemonic: CRESC- means 'grow' in Italian; think of a music swell growing louder.
confusableNote: Often misused to mean the peak itself; technically it is the buildup toward the peak.

definition: Saddened and deflated by disappointment or failure; dejected.
example: The team returned crestfallen after losing the championship in the final seconds.
mnemonic: crestfallen — a bird whose proud crest droops and falls after defeat.

definition: To sing or hum softly and sentimentally in a low, gentle tone.
example: She crooned a lullaby to the restless infant until it finally drifted off to sleep.
mnemonic: CROON: sounds like a quiet, rounded OON — a smooth, round melodious sound.

definition: Large, heavy, or unwieldy; so complicated or burdensome as to be difficult to use.
example: The cumbersome approval process delayed the project by nearly three months.
mnemonic: CUMBER + SOME — a CUCUMBER that's somehow SOME-thing you have to lug around: heavy and awkward.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Having or showing skill in achieving goals through deceit or shrewd manipulation.
example: A cunning strategist, she anticipated her rivals' moves and countered them before they acted.
mnemonic: CUNNING — sounds like 'running' with a C for Clever; always running clever schemes.

definition: To keep under control or within limits; to restrain or moderate.
example: The central bank raised interest rates to curb runaway inflation.
mnemonic: A CURB on a street keeps cars from going too far — curb = keep in bounds.

definition: A bad-tempered, cantankerous person, especially one who is often grumpy and difficult to please.
example: The old curmudgeon next door complained every time neighborhood children played near his fence.
mnemonic: CUR (bad dog) + MUDGEON — a cur in the mud, growling at everyone.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Done hastily and without attention to detail; superficial and careless.
example: The inspector gave the building only a cursory glance before signing off, missing several structural flaws.
mnemonic: CURSOR skims across the screen — a cursory look just skims the surface.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To reduce in extent or quantity; to impose a restriction that cuts something short.
example: Budget cuts forced the university to curtail its study-abroad program significantly.
mnemonic: CURTAIL = cut the TAIL off — shorten or cut back.

definition: Seeming intimidating and difficult enough to discourage or dishearten.
example: Learning a new language in six months seemed daunting, but she committed to daily practice.
mnemonic: daunting sounds like 'don't-ing' — something so scary you want to say 'don't do it.'

definition: To move or act slowly and idly, wasting time; to linger without purpose.
example: If you dawdle every morning, you will be late for every important meeting.
mnemonic: DAWDLE = DOODLE while walking: drawing time out with idle, aimless movement.

definition: A scarcity or inadequate supply of something; a notable lack.
example: The dearth of qualified candidates forced the committee to extend the application deadline by two months.
mnemonic: DEARTH: sounds like 'death' — when something is so scarce it has nearly died out.

definition: To reduce in quality, value, or moral character; to degrade or corrupt.
example: Adding too many alloys debased the gold coins, reducing their worth.
mnemonic: DE + BASE — to bring to a low base, pulling down to the bottom.

definition: Severely weakening the strength, vigor, or functioning of a person or thing.
example: The debilitating illness kept her bedridden for months, unable to work or travel.
mnemonic: DE + BILITY (ability) — strips away your ability, leaving you helpless.

definition: To expose the falseness or exaggerated claims of a myth, belief, or idea.
example: The documentary effectively debunked the widespread myth that humans use only ten percent of their brains.
mnemonic: DEBUNK = remove the BUNK (nonsense) — clear out the false claims.

definition: Characterized by moral decay, self-indulgence, and the abandonment of ethical standards.
example: The decadent aristocracy spent its wealth on lavish parties while the populace starved.
mnemonic: DE-CADE: declining decade by decade into moral ruin.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To succeed in understanding or interpreting something that was obscure or encoded.
example: Scholars spent decades trying to decipher the ancient script found on the cave walls.
mnemonic: DECIPHER = DE (undo) + CIPHER (code) — crack open the coded mystery.

definition: Behavior that conforms to accepted standards of propriety and good taste.
example: The judge reminded spectators that decorum must be maintained throughout the proceedings.
mnemonic: DECORUM = DECOR + um — keeping the 'décor' of social behavior neat and proper.

definition: To publicly denounce or condemn something as wrong, harmful, or failing.
example: Activists decried the new policy as an attack on civil liberties.
mnemonic: DE + CRY — cry out against something publicly, raising your voice in protest.
confusableNote: decry vs. descry: decry means to criticize publicly; descry means to catch sight of something distant.

definition: To damage someone's good reputation through false or malicious statements.
example: The tabloid defamed the actress with fabricated stories about her personal life.
mnemonic: DE + FAME — strip away someone's fame by attacking their name.

definition: Humble submission and respect shown toward someone of authority or superior knowledge.
example: In deference to the elder statesman, the younger delegates allowed him to speak first.
mnemonic: DEFER + ence: to DEFER to someone — yield and step back in respect.
confusableNote: Deference (respectful submission) vs. difference (distinction); and defer (delay) has a separate meaning.

definition: To turn aside, divert, or ward off something, especially criticism or an unwanted course of events.
example: The politician skillfully deflected every accusation by changing the subject to economic growth.
mnemonic: DE + FLECT (bend) — bend it away so it doesn't hit you.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'reflect' (to mirror back) or 'defect' (a flaw or to abandon a cause).

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Neatly skillful and quick in movement or action; nimble and adroit.
example: With deft fingers, the surgeon tied sutures so fine they were nearly invisible.
mnemonic: DEFT — sounds like 'swift and soft'; quick and light-handed.
confusableNote: deft vs. daft — deft means skillful; daft means silly or foolish.

definition: To worship or regard someone as a god; to exalt to a divine or supreme status.
example: Fans deified the rock star to such a degree that every word he spoke was treated as gospel.
mnemonic: Dei (god) + fy (make) — to make someone into a god.

definition: Causing harm or damage, especially in a gradual or subtle way.
example: Prolonged exposure to lead paint has deleterious effects on children's neurological development.
mnemonic: DELETE + rious — deletes your health quietly and completely.

definition: Done with full awareness and careful consideration; intentional and unhurried.
example: His deliberate approach to writing meant every word was chosen with precision and purpose.
mnemonic: LIBRA (scales) in deLIBERate — weighing every option before deciding.
confusableNote: deliberate (adj) = careful and intentional; deliberate (v) = to consider carefully — same spelling, different parts of speech.

definition: To describe, portray, or set out something with precision and clarity.
example: The treaty carefully delineated the boundaries between the two nations' territorial waters.
mnemonic: De-line-ate: draw clear lines around something to define its edges.

definition: A firmly held false belief that persists despite clear contradictory evidence.
example: His delusion that he was a famous poet led him to recite verses at every social gathering, bewildering onlookers.
mnemonic: DE + LUSION (play) — you're being played; reality is an illusion you can't see through.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'illusion' (a perceptual misperception) — a delusion is a fixed false belief, not a sensory trick.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To raise objections or show reluctance; to hesitate or express doubt before agreeing.
example: The attorney demurred when asked to proceed without reviewing the full contract.
mnemonic: de- (down) + mur (murmur) — murmur doubts under your breath before committing.
confusableNote: demure: demure means reserved and modest in manner; demur means to object or hesitate.

definition: To unfairly criticize or belittle someone's reputation or worth.
example: Critics accused the biographer of denigrating the scientist to make his own discoveries seem more original.
mnemonic: DENI-GRATE — grating away at someone's name, reducing them to nothing.

definition: To publicly declare something or someone to be wrong, evil, or worthy of condemnation.
example: The organization denounced the treaty as a violation of international law.
mnemonic: DE + NOUNCE = announce against — making a public announcement to condemn.

definition: The way a person carries and conducts themselves; bearing, posture, and manners.
example: The finishing school prized deportment above academic achievement, training students to walk with books balanced on their heads.
mnemonic: DE-PORT-MENT: how you 'port' (carry) yourself through the world.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'deportation' (forced removal from a country) — deportment concerns behavior, not expulsion.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To remove from power or office, especially by force or legal means.
example: The military deposed the elected president in an early-morning coup.
mnemonic: DE (down) + POSE (position) — to bring someone down from their position.
confusableNote: depose (legal): in law, depose also means to give sworn testimony — context clarifies the sense.

definition: Moral corruption; a state of extreme wickedness or immoral behavior.
example: The tribunal documented the depravity of the regime's treatment of prisoners.
mnemonic: DEPRAVITY = DEPRAVED + ity — the state of being utterly depraved and corrupt.

definition: To express disapproval of something; to belittle or disparage.
example: She deprecated the committee's proposals as half-hearted and insufficiently bold.
mnemonic: DEPRE + cate: depreciate value through words — to talk something down.
confusableNote: deprecate vs. depreciate: deprecate means to express disapproval; depreciate means to decrease in monetary value.

definition: To subject someone or something to contemptuous ridicule or mockery.
example: Critics derided the architect's proposal as impractical and aesthetically offensive.
mnemonic: DE-RIDE: ride over someone, trampling them underfoot with scorn.

definition: Lacking originality; imitated from or based on pre-existing sources rather than being independently created.
example: Critics dismissed the novel as derivative, noting its plot borrowed heavily from earlier dystopian classics.
mnemonic: DERIVE — something derived, not born fresh; it flows from something else.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To dry out completely, removing all moisture; to deprive of vitality.
example: The relentless desert heat desiccated the once-lush vegetation within days.
mnemonic: DE-SICC-ate: 'sicc' from Latin siccus (dry) — de-dry something completely.

definition: Exercising absolute, tyrannical power with no regard for others' rights or freedoms.
example: The despotic ruler imprisoned journalists who dared to criticize his regime.
mnemonic: DESPOT = a tyrant; DESPOTIC describes how a despot rules.

definition: Lacking a clear plan, purpose, or continuity; jumping randomly from one thing to another.
example: The committee's desultory discussion never stayed on topic long enough to produce any useful conclusion.
mnemonic: DESULTORY — like a de-salted, listless person jumping without energy from task to task.

definition: The easing of strained relations between nations or groups through diplomacy.
example: The 1970s détente between the United States and the Soviet Union reduced the immediate threat of nuclear war.
mnemonic: DETENTE: French for 'release of tension' — like releasing a taut string.

definition: To discourage or prevent someone from taking action through fear or doubt.
example: Harsh penalties were introduced to deter would-be offenders from committing fraud.
mnemonic: DETER = DE-TERROR — make something terrifying enough to stop action.

definition: To diminish the worth, value, or importance of something.
example: The grammatical errors in the report detracted from its otherwise compelling argument.
mnemonic: DE + TRACT (pull) — pull away value, leaving less behind.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'distract'; detract means to reduce value, distract means to divert attention.

definition: Causing harm, damage, or injury to someone or something.
example: Smoking is detrimental to cardiovascular health and increases the risk of multiple cancers.
mnemonic: DETRI (worn away) + mental — wears away like gravel grinding something down.

definition: To stray or depart from an established course, norm, or standard; to shift unpredictably.
example: The scientist's results deviated so sharply from the expected model that the team suspected equipment error.
mnemonic: DE + VIA (way) — to leave the way, going off the set path.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Crafty and cunning in a dishonest way; willing to use deceit or underhanded methods to achieve goals.
example: The devious scheme involved forging documents to divert company funds into a private account.
mnemonic: DE + VIA (way) — going off the straight way, taking crooked paths to deceive.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'devout' (deeply religious) or 'obvious' — devious implies deliberate dishonesty.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Showing skill and ease especially in the use of one's hands; mentally agile.
example: The dexterous craftsman carved intricate patterns into the wood with effortless precision.
mnemonic: DEXTER — Latin for 'right hand'; the dexterous person uses their skills like a right hand.

definition: A lengthy, bitter verbal or written attack expressing strong disapproval or anger.
example: The senator launched into a diatribe against the proposed legislation, condemning it as an assault on civil liberties.
mnemonic: DIA (through) + TRIBE — raging through an entire tribe, attacking without end.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: A division into two contrasting or mutually exclusive categories.
example: The philosopher challenged the false dichotomy between reason and emotion.
mnemonic: Dicho- (two) + tomy (cut) — cut cleanly into exactly two opposing parts.

definition: To issue authoritative commands or lay down terms that must be followed.
example: The stronger party was able to dictate the terms of the peace agreement.
mnemonic: DICTATE = DICTATOR behavior — issue commands others must obey.

definition: A formal authoritative pronouncement or a commonly held principle.
example: The judge's dictum that 'justice delayed is justice denied' shaped the court's procedural reforms.
mnemonic: DICT- (Latin: say): a DICTUM is what has been formally SAID.

definition: Intended to teach a moral lesson, sometimes in a heavy-handed or preachy way.
example: The novel's didactic tone grew tiresome as every chapter ended with an explicit moral lecture.
mnemonic: DIDACTIC: TEACH-tic — TEACH + -tic = designed to teach.

definition: Lacking self-confidence; excessively shy or restrained due to self-doubt.
example: Despite her expertise, she remained diffident in meetings, rarely voicing her opinions.
mnemonic: dif- (not) + fident (confident) — the opposite of confident.

definition: Spread out over a wide area; or (of writing/speech) wordy and lacking focus.
example: The professor's lecture was so diffuse that students struggled to identify the central argument.
mnemonic: DIF + FUSE — the fuse spreads out in all directions, losing its concentrated power.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'defuse' (to remove danger from a tense situation) — diffuse means to spread widely.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray
Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: A temporary departure from the main subject in writing or speech, often undermining clarity.
example: The lawyer's digression into ancient Roman law mystified the jury and derailed the proceedings.
mnemonic: DI + GRESS (step) + ION — stepping away from the main path.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Tending to cause delay; slow in acting or making decisions, often deliberately.
example: The opposing counsel's dilatory tactics forced the judge to set strict deadlines for every filing.
mnemonic: DILATORY sounds like 'delay-tory' — someone who delays everything.
confusableNote: dilatory vs. desultory: dilatory means slow/delaying; desultory means scattered/lacking focus.

definition: A person who dabbles in a subject superficially without serious study or commitment.
example: Critics dismissed him as a dilettante who painted for compliments but never mastered the fundamentals.
mnemonic: DILETTANTE — 'delights in' many things; delight without depth.

definition: Extremely small in size; tiny.
example: The diminutive sparrow perched on the windowsill, barely large enough to be visible from across the room.
mnemonic: DIMINUTIVE: DIMINished to the smallest size — diminished all the way.

definition: A mournful song or piece of music composed or performed as a lament for the dead.
example: The brass band played a slow dirge as the funeral procession wound through the streets.
mnemonic: DIRGE: sounds like 'DIRE' — a DIRE song for dark occasions.

definition: Able to be recognized or understood; perceptible to the senses or intellect.
example: After months of tutoring, a discernible improvement in the student's reasoning was finally evident.
mnemonic: DISCERNIBLE = DISCERN + IBLE — if you can discern it, it's clear enough to notice.

definition: A devoted follower who accepts and spreads the teachings or methods of a leader or movement.
example: As a disciple of the Stoic philosophers, she applied their principles of self-control to every challenge.
mnemonic: Disciple shares its root with 'discipline' — a disciple submits to discipline under a teacher.

definition: To cause someone to feel unsettled, embarrassed, or frustrated; to thwart or foil.
example: The unexpected question visibly discomfited the witness, who shifted in his seat and avoided eye contact.
mnemonic: DIS + COMFORT — strip away comfort, leaving someone flustered and ill at ease.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'discomfort' (a noun meaning unease) — discomfit is a verb meaning to actively unsettle someone.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Causing feelings of unease, anxiety, or loss of composure; unsettling.
example: It was disconcerting to find the usually locked laboratory door standing wide open at midnight.
mnemonic: DIS + CONCERT — the concert of calm is broken, throwing everything off-key.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To regard something as unworthy of serious consideration; to dismiss or minimize its importance.
example: Critics were quick to discount the study, citing its small sample size and methodological flaws.
mnemonic: DIS-COUNT — don't count it; remove it from consideration entirely.
confusableNote: discount (GRE trap): commonly means a price reduction, but GRE uses it to mean 'disregard or dismiss' — don't be fooled.

definition: Careful and circumspect in speech or action, especially to avoid causing offense or attracting attention.
example: The diplomat was discreet about the negotiations, revealing nothing to the press.
mnemonic: DISCREET — keep it SECRET; 'discreet' has two e's side by side, quietly together.
confusableNote: Discreet (careful/secretive) vs. discrete (separate/distinct) — both pronounced the same.

definition: A lack of agreement or consistency between facts, figures, or statements.
example: Auditors discovered a discrepancy between the reported revenue and the bank records.
mnemonic: Dis- (apart) + crepare (to rattle) — a rattling gap between two things that don't match.

definition: A feeling that someone or something is unworthy of respect or consideration; contempt.
example: She spoke of the rival school's methods with obvious disdain, barely concealing her contempt.
mnemonic: DIS + DEIGN — the opposite of deigning (condescending to notice); you refuse to even acknowledge them.

definition: To free from entanglement or confusion; to separate out and clarify distinct elements.
example: The biographer struggled to disentangle myth from fact in the legend of the reclusive artist.
mnemonic: DIS + ENTANGLE — undo the tangle; pull the threads apart cleanly.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Not candid or sincere; giving a false impression of honesty or simplicity.
example: It was disingenuous of him to feign surprise at the layoffs he had secretly planned.
mnemonic: DIS + INGENUOUS — the opposite of ingenuous (naive/honest); pretending to be innocent.
confusableNote: Disingenuous (deliberately insincere) vs. ingenuous (honestly naive) — prefix 'dis-' flips the meaning.

definition: Free from personal bias or self-interest; impartial and objective.
example: The arbitrator was chosen specifically because she was disinterested in the outcome of the dispute.
mnemonic: DIS-INTERESTED — strip away personal interest, leaving pure fairness.
confusableNote: disinterested vs. uninterested: disinterested means impartial; uninterested means bored or indifferent.

definition: To take apart piece by piece or systematically destroy a structure or system.
example: The new administration moved swiftly to dismantle the previous era's regulatory framework.
mnemonic: DIS-MANTLE: remove the 'mantle' (covering/structure) piece by piece.

definition: Essentially different in kind; composed of elements that are markedly unlike.
example: The coalition brought together disparate groups united only by their opposition to the policy.
mnemonic: Dis- (apart) + parare (to prepare) — prepared apart, not from the same cloth.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with desperate (frantic with despair); disparate means fundamentally different.

definition: To distribute or supply something; also, to do away with or manage without something.
example: The pharmacist dispensed the medication carefully, while the new policy dispensed with the need for paper forms.
mnemonic: DISPENSE with — spend it out (dis-pense) to others, or spend it away so you no longer need it.
confusableNote: dispense (GRE trap): 'dispense with' means 'do without/eliminate' — the opposite of the distributing sense.

definition: To scatter or cause to spread in different directions over a wide area.
example: Police used loudspeakers to disperse the crowd that had gathered outside the embassy.
mnemonic: DI + SPERSE (sprinkle) — sprinkle in all directions until nothing is concentrated.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'disburse' (to pay out money) — disperse means to scatter physically or spatially.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To conceal one's true motives, feelings, or beliefs under a false appearance.
example: Unwilling to reveal her disappointment, she dissembled with a gracious smile.
mnemonic: DISSEMBLE ≈ DISASSEMBLE the truth — taking it apart and hiding the pieces.
confusableNote: Dissemble (disguise motives) vs. disassemble (take apart physically) — different meanings despite similar sound.

definition: To spread or distribute information, ideas, or material widely to a broad audience.
example: The organization used social media to disseminate public health guidelines during the outbreak.
mnemonic: DISSEMI(N) — like sowing seeds (semin), scattering ideas across the field of public knowledge.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: The holding or expression of opinions that differ from those officially held or accepted.
example: Three justices filed a vigorous dissent, arguing the majority had misinterpreted the constitutional text.
mnemonic: Dis- (apart) + sent (feel) = feeling differently from the group — going your own way.
confusableNote: Dissent (disagreement) vs. 'descent' (going down) — different spellings and meanings.

definition: A person who actively opposes or challenges an established government, policy, or ideology.
example: The dissident was imprisoned for publishing essays critical of the authoritarian regime.
mnemonic: Dissident = one who sits (Latin: sidere) apart from the group — refusing to sit with the mainstream.

definition: To swell or expand outward from internal pressure, especially beyond normal limits.
example: Malnutrition caused the children's abdomens to distend painfully.
mnemonic: DI-STEND: 'stend' from Latin tendere (to stretch) — stretch outward in all directions.
confusableNote: distend vs. extend: distend implies abnormal swelling outward; extend simply means to lengthen.

definition: To extract and present the essential meaning or most important aspects of something complex.
example: The editor distilled the 600-page report into a clear two-page executive summary.
mnemonic: Distill (as in distilling spirits) = purifying a liquid to its purest form — purify ideas to their essence.

definition: To misrepresent or give a misleading account of something; to twist the truth.
example: The propagandist distorted historical events to glorify the regime's founding myths.
mnemonic: DISTORT = DIS + TORT (twist in Latin) — to twist away from the truth.

definition: Experiencing extreme anxiety, sorrow, or suffering.
example: She was clearly distressed by the news of the accident, shaking as she spoke.
mnemonic: distressed = dis- (apart) + stress — stress tearing you apart from within.

definition: Tending to develop in different directions; differing from the norm or from each other.
example: The two scientists reached divergent conclusions from the same dataset.
mnemonic: Di- (apart) + vergere (to turn) — turning away in opposite directions.

definition: Completely separated or detached from something, having no connection to it.
example: His proposal was divorced from reality, ignoring every practical constraint the team faced.
mnemonic: DIVORCED — just like a legal divorce severs a marriage, this severs any connection.
confusableNote: divorced (GRE trap): on GRE means 'separated/detached from' a concept or context, not just marital dissolution.

definition: To make known or reveal information that was previously private or secret.
example: The whistleblower agreed to divulge the company's internal records to the investigative journalists.
mnemonic: DI + VULGE (common, vulgar) — make something known to the common people; bring it into public view.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Rigidly committed to a doctrine or theory without regard for practical considerations.
example: The doctrinaire economist refused to adjust his free-market prescriptions even when evidence showed they were failing.
mnemonic: DOCTRINE + AIRE — an 'air' of absolute devotion to doctrine, no flexibility allowed.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'doctrinal' (simply relating to a doctrine) — doctrinaire carries the negative sense of blind, inflexible adherence.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Based on or consisting of official documents or factual records presented without fictional elements.
example: The documentary evidence presented at trial included receipts, emails, and signed contracts.
mnemonic: DOCUMENT + ARY: of or from actual DOCUMENTS — the facts as recorded.

definition: Having or showing tenacious determination; persisting stubbornly in the face of difficulty.
example: Her dogged pursuit of the truth kept her investigating the case long after others had given up.
mnemonic: DOGGED — like a dog that won't let go of a bone no matter how hard you pull.

definition: Asserting opinions, beliefs, or principles as unquestionably true, without tolerance for doubt or dissent.
example: Her dogmatic insistence on a single methodology alienated colleagues who saw value in alternative approaches.
mnemonic: DOG — a stubborn dog refuses to budge; dogmatic thinkers refuse to budge from their beliefs.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Temporarily inactive or in a suspended state, with potential for future activity.
example: The volcano had been dormant for centuries before it suddenly erupted.
mnemonic: DORM-ant: like sleeping in a dorm — 'dormir' means to sleep in Latin/French.

definition: Unfashionable and dull in appearance; lacking style or attractiveness.
example: She traded her dowdy wardrobe for sleek, modern pieces that transformed her professional image.
mnemonic: DOWDY: think 'down and dull' — a style that is worn-down and uninspiring.

definition: Excessively harsh or severe, especially in laws, rules, or punishments.
example: Critics called the new censorship law draconian, arguing it stifled all legitimate dissent.
mnemonic: From DRACO, the ancient Athenian lawgiver whose laws prescribed death for almost every offense.

definition: Amusingly odd or whimsically humorous in a dry, understated way.
example: His droll commentary on office politics kept colleagues entertained without offending anyone.
mnemonic: DROLL: like a troll who tells quirky, oddly funny jokes.

definition: Hesitantly doubtful; of questionable truth, quality, or legitimacy.
example: The researcher raised dubious objections that his colleagues immediately recognized as unfounded.
mnemonic: DUBIOUS = DUBiOUS — two (duo) minds about whether it's true.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'dubitable,' a rarer synonym; 'indubitable' is its opposite, meaning unquestionable.

definition: To deceive or trick someone into believing something false or acting against their interests.
example: Thousands of retirees were duped into investing their savings in a fraudulent scheme.
mnemonic: DUPE sounds like a dunce cap — someone duped is made into a fool.

definition: Given to or characterized by duplicity; deliberately deceitful in speech and behavior.
example: Her duplicitous manner allowed her to smile warmly at colleagues she was actively undermining.
mnemonic: DUPLICITOUS = DUPLICATE (two copies) — saying one thing while doing another; a double face.

definition: Threats, coercion, or harsh conditions that compel someone to act against their will.
example: The confession was later ruled invalid because it had been signed under duress.
mnemonic: DUR (hard/endure) + ess — a hard, harsh pressure you must endure.

definition: Conscientiously fulfilling one's obligations and responsibilities out of a sense of duty.
example: The dutiful assistant arrived early every morning and stayed late without being asked.
mnemonic: DUTY + FUL — full of duty; carries every obligation to completion.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Gradually decreasing in size, number, or strength; steadily diminishing.
example: The dwindling reserves of fresh water alarmed environmentalists worldwide.
mnemonic: DWINDLE sounds like 'dwindle down' — shrinking toward nothing.

definition: Overflowing with enthusiastic energy, excitement, and high spirits.
example: The ebullient coach leaped off the bench every time his team scored a point.
mnemonic: ebullient shares the root of 'boil' — enthusiasm bubbling over like a boiling pot.

definition: Departing from conventional norms in behavior or outlook in an odd or whimsical way.
example: The eccentric inventor wore a cape to every faculty meeting and kept a live parrot on his shoulder.
mnemonic: EC (out) + CENTER — off-center from the norm, spinning in a different orbit.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: A level or rank within an organization or system; a tier of a hierarchy.
example: She had risen through every echelon of the firm before becoming CEO.
mnemonic: Echelon = a step (French échelle: ladder) — each rung is a different level of rank.

definition: Deriving ideas or styles from a broad and diverse range of sources rather than one tradition.
example: Her eclectic taste in music ranged from Renaissance madrigals to modern electronic compositions.
mnemonic: ECLECTIC: 'ek-legein' (Greek: pick out) — selecting the best from many different sources.

definition: To overshadow or cause to seem inferior; to surpass and render another obscure.
example: Her later achievements eclipsed the early success that had first made her famous.
mnemonic: Like a solar eclipse, one thing blocks and obscures another completely.

definition: Careful, efficient use of resources so that nothing is wasted; sparingness in style or effort.
example: The novelist was admired for her economy of language, conveying complex emotions in very few words.
mnemonic: E-CON-OMY — think ECONOMIZE: using only what's needed, cutting waste.
confusableNote: economy (GRE sense) = efficient use of means/words, NOT the financial/trade system.

definition: To instruct or improve someone morally, intellectually, or spiritually.
example: The documentary was designed to edify viewers about the hidden causes of the financial crisis.
mnemonic: EDI + FY — like 'edifice' (building), you build up a person's mind and character.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'edify' vs 'gratify' — edify means to morally/intellectually improve, not merely to please.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Successful in producing the intended or desired result; effective.
example: The new vaccine proved efficacious in trials, reducing infection rates by over ninety percent.
mnemonic: EFFICACIOUS — EFFICACY + ous; full of efficacy, fully effective.
confusableNote: efficacious vs. efficient — efficacious means it works (produces the result); efficient means it works without wasting resources.

definition: Shameless boldness or brazen impudence; audacious disregard for propriety.
example: He had the effrontery to demand a raise on the very day he was caught falsifying expense reports.
mnemonic: EF + FRONT — putting your worst 'front' forward shamelessly, with no blush of embarrassment.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Believing in or based on the principle that all people are equal and deserve equal rights.
example: The egalitarian reforms eliminated hereditary titles and granted equal voting rights to all citizens.
mnemonic: Egalitarian = 'egal' (French for equal) — rooted in the idea that everyone is equal.

definition: Outstandingly bad or shocking; conspicuously and offensively extreme.
example: The judge called the attorney's misconduct egregious and held him in contempt of court.
mnemonic: E + GREG (flock) — so bad it stands out from the entire flock, unmistakably conspicuous.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To explain or develop something in greater detail; to expand on a point with more information.
example: When asked to elaborate on her thesis, the candidate provided three compelling supporting arguments.
mnemonic: Elaborate = 'labor' inside — you put more work (labor) into explaining something fully.
confusableNote: As an adjective, 'elaborate' means intricate or detailed — a distinct but related sense.

definition: Ecstatically happy and proud, often in response to great success.
example: She was elated when the grant committee approved her full funding request.
mnemonic: elated — lifted up high (e-lated = elevated in spirit).

definition: Of or relating to the most basic level; rudimentary and requiring no advanced knowledge.
example: The professor reminded students that elementary logic should prevent such obvious contradictions in their arguments.
mnemonic: ELEMENT: the elemental, first-level building blocks of a subject.

definition: To draw out or evoke a response, reaction, or information from someone or something.
example: The therapist's careful questioning elicited memories the patient had suppressed for years.
mnemonic: E + LICIT — to legally (licitly) pull something out into the open.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'illicit' (illegal or forbidden) — elicit is a verb meaning to draw out; illicit is an adjective.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Believing that a select group of people with superior attributes deserves privilege and control.
example: Critics argued the admissions policy was elitist, favoring applicants from wealthy, well-connected families.
mnemonic: ELITIST = ELITE + IST — one who worships the elite and scorns those outside that circle.

definition: Fluent, persuasive, and moving in expression, especially in speech.
example: Her eloquent plea for justice moved even the most skeptical members of the committee.
mnemonic: ELOQ(uent): from Latin ELOQUI = to speak out — speaking powerfully OUT.

definition: To make something clear or easy to understand by explaining or illustrating it.
example: The professor used vivid diagrams to elucidate the otherwise abstract principles of thermodynamics.
mnemonic: ELUCIDATE = e-LUCID-ate — bring LUCIDITY (clarity) to something murky.

definition: Difficult to find, define, capture, or grasp; tending to evade pursuit or understanding.
example: True happiness proved elusive for the philosopher who had devoted his life to studying it.
mnemonic: E + LUSIVE (elude) — always eluding your grasp, slipping away just as you reach.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'allusive' (making indirect references) or 'illusive' (based on illusion).

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To make something more attractive or interesting by adding decorative details, often exaggerating the truth.
example: The travel writer embellished her account of the journey with vivid but invented episodes of adventure.
mnemonic: EMBELLISH = EM + BELL — hang bells on something to make it look festive and fancy.

definition: Showing the ability to understand and share the feelings and experiences of another person.
example: An empathetic counselor listens without judgment and validates the client's emotions.
mnemonic: Empathy = feeling 'in' (em-) another's 'pathos' (feelings).
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'sympathetic,' which implies feeling sorry for someone rather than truly sharing their experience.

definition: Based on observation and experiment rather than theory or pure logic alone.
example: The hypothesis gained credibility only after empirical studies confirmed the predicted outcomes.
mnemonic: Empirical = from 'empiric' (a practitioner who relies on experience) — grounded in real-world experience.
confusableNote: Empirical contrasts with 'theoretical' — empirical knowledge comes from data, not pure reasoning.

definition: To imitate or strive to match or surpass someone admired, typically by copying their methods.
example: Young athletes around the world emulate the training regimen of the legendary sprinter.
mnemonic: EMU — an emu can't fly but tries to emulate birds that can; striving to match the ideal.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'simulate' (to replicate without necessarily aspiring to match) — emulate implies admiration and competitive aspiration.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To fill someone with intense delight; to charm or captivate completely.
example: The storyteller's voice enchanted the audience, holding them rapt for hours.
mnemonic: enchant = en- (into) + chant — to sing someone into a delighted trance.

definition: To intrude gradually on another's territory, rights, or domain beyond acceptable limits.
example: Urban development began to encroach on the protected wetlands despite environmental regulations.
mnemonic: EN + CROAK — creeping like a frog into territory that isn't yours.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Covering or exhibiting a wide range of information; comprehensive in knowledge.
example: Her encyclopedic knowledge of botany allowed her to identify every plant in the forest by genus.
mnemonic: ENCYCLOPEDIA — like a walking encyclopedia; knows something about everything.

definition: Regularly found and persistently established within a particular area or group.
example: Corruption had become endemic to the region's political institutions over decades.
mnemonic: EN + DEMOS (people) — native to and constant among a specific people or place.
confusableNote: endemic vs. epidemic: endemic means consistently present in a region; epidemic means suddenly spreading widely.

definition: To provide with a quality, ability, or asset; to furnish with a lasting gift or fund.
example: The scholarship endowed promising students with financial support throughout their education.
mnemonic: EN + DOW: put a DOWry in — gift someone with lasting resources.

definition: To drain of vitality, strength, or vigor; to weaken physically or mentally.
example: The oppressive tropical heat enervated the expedition team, forcing them to rest during midday hours.
mnemonic: E + NERVE — remove the nerve/energy from someone, leaving them limp and weak.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'energize' (to invigorate) — enervate is the opposite, meaning to drain energy.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To give rise to or bring about a feeling, situation, or condition.
example: Years of broken promises engendered deep distrust between the two communities.
mnemonic: EN-GENDER: to 'give birth to' something — from Latin generare (to beget).

definition: Difficult to interpret or understand; mysterious and puzzling in character or appearance.
example: The Mona Lisa's enigmatic smile has inspired centuries of speculation about its true meaning.
mnemonic: ENIGMATIC = ENIGMA + TIC — puzzles keep ticking away without a solution.

definition: Active and deeply felt hostility or hatred between people or groups.
example: The two families harbored enmity for generations after a disputed land claim.
mnemonic: ENEMY + ity — the state of being someone's enemy.

definition: A feeling of listless weariness and dissatisfaction arising from lack of engagement or excitement.
example: After months of repetitive tasks, a creeping ennui made even simple work feel unbearable.
mnemonic: ennui (French for boredom) — imagine a bored Parisian sighing 'en-WEE' at everything.

definition: To happen or occur afterward as a consequence or result.
example: After the controversial verdict, heated protests ensued across the capital.
mnemonic: EN-SUE: think 'in sequence' — it comes right after something else.
confusableNote: ensue vs. issue: ensue means to follow as a consequence; issue can mean to emerge or a problem.

definition: To capture and hold someone's fascinated attention completely.
example: The documentary enthralled viewers with never-before-seen footage of deep-sea creatures.
mnemonic: enthrall = en- (put into) + thrall (slavery) — enslaved by fascination.

definition: Believing oneself inherently deserving of privileges or special treatment without earning them.
example: The entitled executive expected staff to cater to his every whim regardless of how unreasonable the request.
mnemonic: ENTITLED — acting as if a title of royalty grants you everything automatically.
confusableNote: entitled (GRE trap): commonly used to mean 'having a legal right,' but GRE tests the pejorative sense of unwarranted self-importance.

definition: To ask someone earnestly and urgently; to plead or implore.
example: The refugees entreated border officials to allow them safe passage to the neighboring country.
mnemonic: EN + TREAT — treat someone to an earnest appeal, giving your emotions fully to the request.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Firmly established and difficult to change, as if dug into defensive positions.
example: Entrenched attitudes about gender roles proved stubbornly resistant to reform.
mnemonic: Like soldiers in a TRENCH — deeply dug in and hard to dislodge.

definition: Lasting only a very short time; transitory.
example: Social media fame is often ephemeral, fading within days of a viral moment.
mnemonic: EPHEMERA = things lasting only a day (Greek hemera = day); think mayfly, living one day.

definition: Mental calmness and composure, especially under stress or adversity.
example: She faced the harsh critique with equanimity, calmly noting which points had merit.
mnemonic: equi- (equal) + anima (spirit) — keeping your spirit equally balanced at all times.

definition: Fair and impartial; treating all parties justly and without favoritism.
example: The mediator proposed an equitable division of assets that satisfied both sides.
mnemonic: Equity = fairness; equitable = characterized by equity.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'equal'; 'equitable' means fair/just, not necessarily identical treatment.

definition: To use deliberately vague language to avoid commitment or to mislead.
example: The politician equivocated for ten minutes without ever stating a clear position on the tax plan.
mnemonic: equi- (equal) + voc (voice) — speaking equally in two directions, committing to neither.

definition: To completely destroy or eliminate something, especially something harmful, so it no longer exists.
example: Global health campaigns succeeded in eradicating smallpox by the late twentieth century.
mnemonic: E-RADIC-ate: 'radic' from Latin radix (root) — pull out by the roots entirely.

definition: Inconsistent and unpredictable in movement, behavior, or quality; not following a regular pattern.
example: The erratic driver swerved between lanes without signaling, alarming everyone nearby.
mnemonic: ERRATIC = ERR + ATIC: constantly erring in direction — wandering off any stable path.

definition: Former; belonging to an earlier time; previously holding a certain status.
example: The erstwhile rivals surprised everyone by announcing a joint business venture.
mnemonic: ERST-while: 'erst' is an archaic word for 'formerly' — 'erstwhile' = 'at a former time.'

definition: Having or showing great knowledge acquired through extensive reading and study.
example: The erudite professor peppered her lectures with references ranging from Aristotle to Zhuangzi.
mnemonic: ERUDITE — 'e-rude' reversed; un-rough; polished by learning.

definition: To increase rapidly in intensity, magnitude, or scope.
example: What began as a minor dispute quickly escalated into a full diplomatic crisis.
mnemonic: ESCAL-ate: like climbing an escalator — steadily rising higher.

definition: To deliberately abstain from or avoid something, especially on moral or practical grounds.
example: After years of stress, he eschewed corporate life in favor of farming.
mnemonic: Eschew sounds like 'ah-choo' — sneeze away anything you want to avoid.

definition: Intended for or understood by only a small group with specialized or secret knowledge.
example: The professor's lecture on esoteric medieval alchemy drew only a handful of specialists.
mnemonic: Esoteric = 'inner circle' (Greek esōterikos) — kept within, not for outsiders.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with exoteric, which means suitable for the general public.

definition: High regard and respectful admiration held for a person or thing.
example: She was held in high esteem by colleagues who admired her integrity and expertise.
mnemonic: Esteem = 'estimate' someone's worth highly.

definition: Worthy of great respect and admiration; deserving esteem.
example: Her estimable contributions to public health were honored with a lifetime achievement award.
mnemonic: Estimable = 'estimate' as highly valuable — worthy of high estimation.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'estimatable' (able to be estimated numerically); 'estimable' means worthy of admiration.

definition: No longer close or affectionate; having become distant or hostile in a relationship.
example: After the bitter legal battle over the inheritance, the siblings became estranged for decades.
mnemonic: ESTRANGED: contains STRANGE — someone who was close now feels like a stranger.

definition: A mild or indirect expression used in place of one that might seem blunt or offensive.
example: 'Passed away' is a common euphemism for 'died,' softening the harsh reality of death.
mnemonic: Eu (good) + pheme (speech) = 'good speech' — substituting a 'nicer' word for an uncomfortable one.

definition: Feeling an intense state of happiness or excitement, often disproportionate to circumstances.
example: Finishing the marathon left him euphoric, grinning and weeping simultaneously.
mnemonic: eu- (good/well) + phoric (bearing) — carrying an overwhelming sense of well-being.

definition: To escape or avoid something by cunning or deceitful means; to dodge the truth.
example: The suspect evaded every direct question with vague, carefully worded non-answers.
mnemonic: EVADE = E + WADE — wade sideways out of the path of truth.
confusableNote: Evade (deliberately avoid/deceive) vs. avoid (merely stay away from) — evade implies cunning.

definition: Quickly fading or disappearing; lasting only a brief time; transitory.
example: The evanescent beauty of cherry blossoms makes their brief season all the more treasured.
mnemonic: EVAN-escent: 'evan' from Latin evanescere (to vanish) — evaporating into nothing.

definition: Tending to avoid commitment, directness, or honesty; deliberately vague or misleading.
example: His evasive responses during the hearing convinced the committee he was hiding something.
mnemonic: EVASIVE — like someone always 'evading' your grasp; slippery and hard to pin down.

definition: To call forth or bring to mind a feeling, memory, or image.
example: The old photograph evoked a wave of nostalgia for his childhood summers.
mnemonic: E-VOKE: 'voke' from Latin vocare (to call) — call something out from within.
confusableNote: evoke vs. invoke: evoke means to call forth (a feeling/memory); invoke means to call upon (authority/law).

definition: To make a problem, condition, or situation worse or more severe.
example: The drought exacerbated the famine, turning a food shortage into a humanitarian catastrophe.
mnemonic: EXACER(B) — sounds like 'exasperate'; both make bad things worse.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'exasperate' (to irritate greatly) — exacerbate means to worsen a situation or condition, not just to annoy.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Demanding strict adherence to standards; requiring great care, effort, and precision.
example: The exacting editor returned manuscripts with hundreds of margin notes and corrections.
mnemonic: EXACTING = EXACT + -ing — demanding everything be exactly right.

definition: To praise or honor highly; to elevate in rank, character, or esteem.
example: The biography exalted the scientist as one of the greatest minds of the twentieth century.
mnemonic: Exalt = ex (up) + alt (high) — to lift someone up high in praise.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'exult,' which means to feel triumphant joy.

definition: Intensely irritated and frustrated, especially by repeated annoyances.
example: The editor grew exasperated as the writer submitted the same errors for a third time.
mnemonic: exasperated — ex- (out) + asper (rough) — so irritated your rough edges show.

definition: To criticize someone severely and scathingly, as if stripping away their skin.
example: The review excoriated the novelist's latest book as derivative and intellectually dishonest.
mnemonic: EX-CORIATE: from 'corium' (skin) — to strip the skin off; brutal verbal flaying.

definition: To clear someone from blame or fault; to officially absolve of wrongdoing.
example: New DNA evidence exculpated the prisoner who had spent twelve years behind bars for a crime he did not commit.
mnemonic: EX + CULPA (Latin: fault) — remove the fault, declare the person free of blame.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'exonerate' — they are near-synonyms, but exculpate specifically removes culpability; exonerate also lifts a burden of obligation.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Critical explanation or scholarly interpretation of a text, especially a religious scripture.
example: The theologian's exegesis of the passage revealed layers of meaning invisible to casual readers.
mnemonic: EXE-GESIS: EX (out) + HEGESIS (lead) — lead OUT the meaning from the text.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'eisegesis' — exegesis draws meaning out of the text; eisegesis reads meaning into it.

definition: Thorough and complete to the fullest extent; leaving nothing out.
example: The commission conducted an exhaustive review of every document before reaching its verdict.
mnemonic: EXHAUSTIVE: EXHAUSTs every possibility — covers everything until nothing remains.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'exhausted' (tired); exhaustive means thorough and complete, not fatigued.

definition: Making one feel thrilled, invigorated, and full of excited energy.
example: The first solo skydive was an exhilarating experience she would never forget.
mnemonic: exhilarating = ex- (out) + hilar (cheerful) — cheerfulness bursting outward.

definition: To strongly urge or encourage someone to take a particular action through earnest appeals.
example: The coach exhorted his players to give their best effort in the final minutes of the championship game.
mnemonic: EX + HORT (garden) — cultivate action in others, pushing growth through persuasion.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'extort' (to obtain by threats) — exhort relies on moral persuasion, not coercion.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Requiring immediate attention or action due to pressing urgency; demanding and critical.
example: The exigent circumstances of the flood left officials no time to convene a formal committee.
mnemonic: EXIT + GENT — the urgency demands an exit right now; no time to wait.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'exigent' vs 'stringent' (strict/demanding) — exigent stresses urgency; stringent stresses severity of standards.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To officially free someone from blame, guilt, or a burdensome obligation.
example: The court's ruling exonerated the company from any liability in the accident.
mnemonic: EX + ONUS (burden) — remove the burden/onus of blame from the accused.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Unreasonably high or excessive, far beyond what is considered normal or fair.
example: Tenants protested the exorbitant rent increases that priced working families out of the neighborhood.
mnemonic: EXORBITANT = EX + ORBIT — so far out it's gone out of orbit, beyond all reasonable bounds.

definition: To expel or eliminate something troubling or malevolent; to rid oneself of a harmful influence.
example: Writing her memoir helped her exorcise the demons of a difficult childhood.
mnemonic: EX-ORC-ise: 'ex' (out) + force the bad spirit out — casting out evil.
confusableNote: exorcise vs. exercise: exorcise means to drive out evil/trouble; exercise means physical activity or to practice.

definition: Strikingly unusual or strange because it originates from a distant or foreign place; unfamiliar and intriguing.
example: The market was filled with exotic spices that locals had never seen before the trade routes opened.
mnemonic: EXO (outside) — coming from outside the known world, strange and alluring.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To speak or write at great length or in elaborate detail on a subject.
example: The professor was known to expatiate on minor points until students lost the thread of the lecture.
mnemonic: EX-SPATIATE: to SPATIALLY expand — spread your words out widely.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'expatriate' (to live abroad); expatiate means to speak at length.

definition: Convenient and practical for a particular purpose, often with an implication of self-interest over principle.
example: The politician's sudden change of stance was clearly expedient rather than driven by genuine conviction.
mnemonic: EXPEDIENT = EXPEDIENTly practical — it 'expedites' a goal, chosen for speed over ethics.
confusableNote: Expedient often implies moral compromise; 'practical' is neutral. Expedient = practical but possibly unprincipled.

definition: To make a process happen more quickly; to speed up or accelerate the progress of something.
example: The agency promised to expedite the visa application so the student could arrive before the semester began.
mnemonic: EX-PEDITE — free the feet (ped = foot); remove obstacles so it can move fast.

definition: Capable of being accounted for or explained in understandable terms.
example: The scientist argued that all seemingly supernatural events were ultimately explicable through natural laws.
mnemonic: EXPLICABLE = EXPLAIN + ABLE — it CAN be explained, made plain.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'inexplicable,' its opposite, meaning impossible to explain.

definition: Making use of a situation or person in an unfair, selfish, or immoral way for one's own gain.
example: Labor advocates condemned the factory's exploitative practices, including unpaid overtime and unsafe conditions.
mnemonic: EXPLOIT + ATIVE — turning people into resources purely for your own benefit.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Still in existence; not destroyed, lost, or extinct.
example: Only three extant manuscripts of the medieval poem have survived to the present day.
mnemonic: EX-TANT: 'ex' (out/standing) + 'tant' — still standing out there, not gone.
confusableNote: extant vs. extent: extant means still existing; extent refers to the range or scope of something.

definition: To compose or perform something without preparation; to improvise on the spot.
example: When the keynote speaker canceled, the host extemporized a forty-minute talk from memory.
mnemonic: EX + TEMPORE (Latin: out of the moment) — speaking straight out of the present moment, no preparation.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Making a wrongful act or offense seem less serious by providing partial justification or mitigating context.
example: The judge considered the defendant's abusive childhood as an extenuating circumstance in sentencing.
mnemonic: EXTENUAT(E) — extend mercy by thinning (attenuating) the severity of the fault.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'exacerbating' (worsening) — extenuating circumstances lessen blame, not increase it.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To praise enthusiastically and at length; to speak in the highest terms of.
example: Health advocates extol the virtues of a Mediterranean diet at every opportunity.
mnemonic: Extol = 'extra toll of praise' — going above and beyond in praising.

definition: Irrelevant or unrelated to the subject at hand; not essential.
example: The editor cut all extraneous details from the report, leaving only data directly relevant to the findings.
mnemonic: EXTRANEOUS: EXTRA things that don't belong — tossed in from outside the subject.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'extrinsic' (coming from outside); extraneous specifically means irrelevant or unnecessary.

definition: To extend known data or conclusions into an unknown area by assuming established trends continue.
example: From the current growth rate, analysts extrapolated that the company would double revenue within five years.
mnemonic: Extra-polate: go extra (beyond) the data poles — extend the graph beyond the known points.
confusableNote: Extrapolate (projecting beyond known data) differs from 'interpolate' (estimating within known data points).

definition: Spending much more than necessary or proper; exceeding the bounds of reason in style or scale.
example: The CEO's extravagant lifestyle — private jets and a fleet of luxury cars — alienated shareholders during hard times.
mnemonic: EXTRA + VAGANT (wander) — wandering far beyond the bounds of what is reasonable or needed.

definition: To invent or concoct something false, especially a story or evidence.
example: The witness admitted she had fabricated her alibi under pressure from the defendant.
mnemonic: FABRICATE = FABRIC + ate — weaving a cloth of lies from thread and air.
confusableNote: Fabricate (make up/lie) vs. manufacture (make legitimately) — fabricate implies falsification in GRE contexts.

definition: Treating serious issues with inappropriate humor; flippantly joking.
example: His facetious remark about the disaster relief effort drew sharp criticism from the audience.
mnemonic: FACE + tious — putting on a joking face at the wrong moment.
confusableNote: facetious vs. sarcastic: facetious implies playful, irreverent humor; sarcastic implies cutting, bitter mockery.

definition: An exceptional natural ability to do something with ease and fluency.
example: She had a remarkable facility for languages, becoming conversational in Japanese within six months.
mnemonic: FACILE + ity — facile means easy; facility = natural ease in doing something.
confusableNote: facility (ability sense) vs. facility (building sense) — on the GRE, facility often means ease/skill, not a physical building.

definition: Relating to or promoting internal dissension and conflict within a group.
example: The factious political party could not unify around a single candidate.
mnemonic: FACTION + ious — full of factions fighting each other from within.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'fractious' (irritable/unruly) or 'fictitious' (imaginary).

definition: Based on a mistaken belief or unsound reasoning; logically misleading or deceptive.
example: The senator's fallacious claim that crime had risen was quickly disproved by official statistics.
mnemonic: FALLACIOUS = FALLACY + OUS — full of logical falls and stumbles.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'fallible,' which means capable of error, not necessarily deceptive.

definition: Capable of making errors or being wrong; not perfectly reliable or infallible.
example: Even the most experienced judges are fallible, which is why appellate courts exist.
mnemonic: FALLIBLE = prone to FALL — can stumble and make mistakes.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'fallacious,' which means based on false reasoning rather than merely prone to error.

definition: Inactive, dormant, or uncultivated; (of land) left unplanted to recover fertility.
example: After years of frantic productivity, she let her creative work lie fallow for a season.
mnemonic: FALLOW field: farmers leave fields 'fellow-less' (without crops) to rest and recover.

definition: To lose strength, momentum, or confidence and begin to fail or hesitate.
example: Her resolve faltered under the relentless pressure of the final examination.
mnemonic: FALTER = FALL + hesitATER — you fall into hesitation.

definition: Imaginative and inventive in an unrestrained, creative, or whimsical way; not based on reality.
example: The architect's fanciful design featured towers shaped like giant seashells spiraling toward the sky.
mnemonic: FANCY + FUL — full of fancy; the imagination runs freely without constraint.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'fanciful' (creatively imaginative) and 'derivative' (imitative) — they are near-opposites in the originality sense.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Excessively attentive to accuracy and detail; very hard to please due to high standards.
example: A fastidious proofreader, she caught every misplaced comma in the hundred-page document.
mnemonic: FASTIDIOUS — if it's not FAULTLESS, a fastidious person is DISGUSTED.

definition: To grasp the full meaning or depth of something difficult or complex.
example: Many students struggle to fathom the implications of Gödel's incompleteness theorems.
mnemonic: FATHOM = measure the DEPTH (a fathom is 6 feet deep) — get to the bottom of it.

definition: To seek favor by flattering or behaving in an excessively submissive, obsequious way.
example: The new intern fawned over the CEO, laughing at every joke and agreeing with every opinion.
mnemonic: FAWN like a puppy — tail wagging, desperately seeking approval.
confusableNote: Fawn (v. to flatter submissively) vs. fawn (n. a young deer); the GRE tests the verb meaning.

definition: Capable of being done or carried out successfully; practically possible.
example: Engineers determined that constructing a bridge across the strait was technically feasible within the budget.
mnemonic: FEASIBLE = CAN be DONE — think 'fais' (French: do); if it's feasible, you can do it.

definition: Lacking initiative, drive, or responsibility; ineffective and irresponsible.
example: The feckless committee member never completed assignments and missed half of the scheduled meetings.
mnemonic: FECK (effect) + LESS — without effect; producing nothing of value.

definition: Highly productive or fertile, whether in generating offspring, ideas, or creative output.
example: The fecund novelist published seven books in a single decade, each exploring a different literary form.
mnemonic: FECUND: sounds like 'fecundity' — the rich soil of fertility producing abundantly.

definition: Lacking physical strength, force, or effectiveness; ineffectually weak.
example: The senator's feeble counterargument failed to sway even his own supporters.
mnemonic: FEEBLE sounds like FEEBLE-minded: weak in body or argument.

definition: To pretend to feel or be affected by something; to simulate falsely.
example: He feigned ignorance of the policy violations, though the emails proved he had been fully informed.
mnemonic: FEIGN rhymes with FAKE — to fake a feeling or condition convincingly.

definition: A deceptive movement or action intended to distract an opponent from one's real intention.
example: The general's assault on the eastern flank was a feint designed to draw forces away from the real attack.
mnemonic: FEINT = FAINT — you pretend (feign) to faint to distract while you act elsewhere.
confusableNote: Feint (deceptive maneuver) vs. faint (lose consciousness or barely perceptible) — same pronunciation, different meaning.

definition: Well-chosen and pleasingly apt; agreeably suited to the occasion or context.
example: The poet's felicitous choice of imagery made the description feel effortlessly precise.
mnemonic: FELICITY (happiness/aptness): a felicitous phrase 'fits' beautifully and brings satisfaction.
confusableNote: felicitous vs. felicitious: 'felicitious' is not a word — only felicitous is correct.

definition: Having or showing deeply felt, earnest enthusiasm or belief.
example: Her fervent plea for justice moved many in the courtroom to tears.
mnemonic: fervent shares the root ferv- (to boil) — boiling with passion.
confusableNote: fervent vs. fervid: fervid implies more excessive, almost uncontrolled intensity; fervent is earnest and sincere.

definition: Intensely passionate to the point of excess; excessively ardent.
example: His fervid devotion to the cause made even his allies uncomfortable.
mnemonic: fervid = ferv- (boil) + -id — something so boiling hot it's almost out of control.
confusableNote: fervid vs. fervent: fervid suggests more excessive, unrestrained heat than fervent.

definition: Intense, deeply felt enthusiasm or passion for a belief or cause.
example: The crowd cheered with patriotic fervor as the team won the championship.
mnemonic: fervor = ferv- (boil) — the heat of boiling passion for something.

definition: To become worse or more intense, especially through neglect; to cause continuing bitterness or resentment.
example: The unresolved grievance festered for years until it finally erupted in a bitter public dispute.
mnemonic: FESTER — a wound left unattended festers; hidden problems rot from within.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Changing frequently and unpredictably in loyalty, affection, or opinion; not steadfast.
example: The fickle voters who supported the reform movement in March had largely abandoned it by autumn.
mnemonic: FICKLE rhymes with PICKLE — stuck in a changing brine, shifting flavor all the time.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Using figures of speech; not literal but expressing ideas through imagery or metaphor.
example: When she said her heart was broken, she meant it in the figurative sense, not a medical one.
mnemonic: FIGURE-ative: uses FIGURES of speech — shaped by imagery rather than plain fact.
confusableNote: Contrasts with 'literal'; figurative language uses comparison, imagery, or symbolism.

definition: Excessively particular about minor details; difficult to please due to excessive fussiness.
example: The finicky editor returned the manuscript three times, each time finding new errors too trivial to mention.
mnemonic: FINICKY — like a picky fin(ger), poking at every tiny flaw in search of perfection.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Occurring in irregular bursts; not steady or continuous.
example: She managed only fitful sleep, waking every hour through the stormy night.
mnemonic: FIT + FUL — coming in fits and starts, not smoothly.

definition: Conspicuously and shockingly obvious in a bad or harmful way; glaringly offensive.
example: The referee's flagrant disregard for the rules drew outrage from both teams.
mnemonic: FLAMEgrant — it burns so obviously everyone can see it.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'fragrant' (having a pleasant smell); 'flagrant' signals moral offensiveness, not aroma.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Strikingly bold, showy, or colorful in a way that attracts attention; ostentatiously elaborate.
example: The designer's flamboyant runway collection drew gasps — sequined capes and ten-inch platform heels.
mnemonic: FLAMBOYANT = FLAME + BUOYANT — like a flame, bright and dancing, impossible to ignore.

definition: New and inexperienced; just beginning to develop or emerge.
example: The fledgling startup struggled to compete against established industry giants.
mnemonic: FLEDG-ling: a baby bird just growing feathers (fledge) — barely able to fly yet.

definition: Excessively elaborate or showy in style, language, or appearance.
example: The senator's florid rhetoric impressed some listeners but struck others as overwrought and hollow.
mnemonic: FLORID = full of floral excess — too many flowers on the page.

definition: To openly and contemptuously disregard or violate a rule, law, or convention.
example: The corporation flouted environmental regulations for decades before being fined.
mnemonic: Flout rhymes with 'out' — you throw the rules OUT with contempt.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'flaunt' (to display ostentatiously); flouting breaks rules, flaunting shows off.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Completely bewildered and at a loss; utterly perplexed and unable to proceed.
example: The engineer was flummoxed when the prototype failed despite every calculation being correct.
mnemonic: FLUMMOXED = sounds like FLUM(ped) + FOXED — tricked and knocked down flat.

definition: Having plentiful resources, especially money; also, reddened in the face from embarrassment or heat.
example: After the successful IPO, the startup was flush with cash and began hiring aggressively.
mnemonic: FLUSH — like a full flush of cards, holding plenty; or a face flush (red) with emotion.
confusableNote: flush (GRE trap): GRE tests 'flush with cash' (abundant/well-supplied) and 'flush with embarrassment' (reddened), not the plumbing sense.

definition: Agitated, confused, and nervously upset, often causing disorganized behavior.
example: She became flustered when the interviewer changed the topic without warning.
mnemonic: flustered — like fluttering wings in a panic, unable to settle down.

definition: To prevent someone from succeeding or to thwart a plan or attempt.
example: The detective foiled the smuggler's plan before the contraband crossed the border.
mnemonic: Tin FOIL wraps things up and stops them — it foils your plans.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To instigate or stir up trouble, rebellion, or discord; to incite unrest.
example: The demagogue used inflammatory rhetoric to foment resentment among the populace.
mnemonic: FO-MENT: think 'ferment' — like fermenting trouble, making it bubble and brew.

definition: Recklessly bold or rash; taking unnecessary risks without good judgment.
example: It was foolhardy to attempt the mountain climb in a snowstorm without proper equipment.
mnemonic: FOOL + HARDY — only a fool would be hardy (bold) in that situation.
confusableNote: foolhardy vs. bold — bold implies courage with judgment; foolhardy implies bravery without sense.

definition: To restrain oneself from acting on an impulse; to show patient self-control or tolerance.
example: She forbore from criticizing her colleague's work, choosing diplomacy over candor.
mnemonic: FOR-BEAR: bear it (endure) for now — hold back and be patient.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'forebear' (an ancestor); 'forbear' (verb) means to hold back.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To be an advance warning or sign of something bad; to predict evil.
example: Dark clouds on the horizon seemed to forebode a disastrous voyage ahead.
mnemonic: FORE (before) + BODE (omen) — boding something bad before it happens.

definition: Able to be anticipated or predicted within a reasonable future horizon.
example: Climate scientists warn that sea levels will continue rising for the foreseeable future.
mnemonic: FORE (ahead) + SEE — what you can see coming ahead of time.

definition: To prevent or obstruct something by taking advance action before it can occur.
example: She made a preemptive announcement to forestall any damaging rumors from spreading.
mnemonic: FORE (before) + STALL — stall something before it even starts moving.

definition: To abstain from or give up something desirable voluntarily.
example: He chose to forgo the bonus in order to maintain his ethical standards.
mnemonic: FORGO = FOR + GO — let it go, don't take it.
confusableNote: forego: forego means to precede or go before; forgo means to do without — easily confused.

definition: To abandon or renounce someone or something once held dear.
example: He forsook his comfortable career to pursue a life of humanitarian service.
mnemonic: FOR + SAKE — 'for whose sake do you leave?' — you leave it behind.

definition: To strengthen or reinforce against attack or weakness, physically or figuratively.
example: A good mentor can fortify a student's confidence during the most difficult periods of study.
mnemonic: FORT + ify: build a FORT — make it stronger against assault.

definition: Courage and mental strength to endure pain, adversity, or danger without complaint.
example: The survivors demonstrated remarkable fortitude during months of harsh conditions in the wilderness.
mnemonic: FORT — a strong fortress inside you that withstands any attack.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Happening by a lucky chance; occurring accidentally but with a fortunate outcome.
example: Their fortuitous meeting at the conference led to a collaboration that revolutionized the field.
mnemonic: FORTUITOUS contains FORTUNE — a fortunate accident, luck's gift without planning.
confusableNote: Fortuitous strictly means 'by chance,' not necessarily 'lucky,' though GRE usage favors the fortunate sense.
