definition: Irritable, unruly, and prone to quarreling or causing trouble.
example: The fractious toddler refused every meal and threw a tantrum at each suggestion.
mnemonic: FRACTURE + ious — like something fractured, always breaking apart in irritation.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'factious' (promoting internal group conflict); fractious emphasizes irritability and unruliness.

definition: The condition of being weak and delicate in body or character; a moral or physical weakness.
example: The philosopher argued that human frailty makes absolute virtue an unreachable ideal.
mnemonic: FRAILTY = FRAIL + -ty: the state of being easily broken like a frail twig.

definition: Open and direct in speech; not afraid to say what one thinks, even bluntly.
example: To be frank, the research methodology has serious flaws that invalidate the conclusions.
mnemonic: FRANK: think of a friend named Frank who always tells you the honest truth.

definition: Filled with or causing anxiety, distress, or risk; laden with something undesirable.
example: The negotiations were fraught with tension, as neither side was willing to make the first concession.
mnemonic: FRAUGHT = FREIGHT — heavy freight weighs you down; fraught situations weigh on you with danger.

definition: Wildly excited or uncontrolled; characterized by rapid, disordered, frantic activity.
example: The trading floor was frenetic in the final minutes before the market closed.
mnemonic: FRENETIC sounds like 'frenzy-tic' — twitching with uncontrollable energy.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To feel persistent, often excessive anxiety or worry about something.
example: He would fret for hours over minor details that others dismissed without thought.
mnemonic: fret sounds like the frets on a guitar — constantly pressing and fretting over every note.

definition: The outer or less important margin of a group, activity, or subject; a peripheral or extreme position.
example: The theory remained on the fringe of academic debate, rejected by most mainstream scholars.
mnemonic: FRINGE: the decorative edge of a rug — far from the center, easily overlooked.

definition: Lacking seriousness or importance; trivially weak or without merit.
example: The judge dismissed the frivolous lawsuit, noting it had no factual basis whatsoever.
mnemonic: FRIVolous — full of friv(ol)ity, light as bubbles, nothing weighty inside.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Producing good or abundant results; highly productive or beneficial.
example: The partnership proved fruitful, yielding three joint publications in the first year alone.
mnemonic: FRUITFUL: a tree that bears much FRUIT — effort that yields rich results.

definition: The central support point of a lever; figuratively, the key pivot or crucial element enabling change.
example: Access to capital was the fulcrum on which the entire startup's success balanced.
mnemonic: Full-CRUM: the crumb at the full center — the one point holding everything up.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Interchangeable with other individual items of the same kind; mutually substitutable.
example: Money is fungible — one dollar bill is perfectly substitutable for any other.
mnemonic: Fungi- from Latin fungi (to perform) — each unit performs identically, so swap freely.

definition: Attempting to avoid notice or attention; secretive and sly in manner.
example: She cast furtive glances at the door, clearly anxious about being overheard.
mnemonic: FURTIVE = FUR (a thief wears soft fur to move silently) + tive — sneaking in silence.

definition: Incapable of producing any useful result; utterly pointless or ineffective.
example: It proved futile to argue with the committee once they had already reached a unanimous decision.
mnemonic: FUTILE = FEW + TILE — so few tiles you can't build anything; the effort leads nowhere.

definition: An embarrassing social blunder or tactless remark made in a public or formal setting.
example: The ambassador's gaffe at the dinner — confusing the two countries' names — made headlines.
mnemonic: GAFFE sounds like 'goof' — you goofed in front of everyone.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To deny or contradict; to speak against or oppose a claim.
example: No one could gainsay the evidence presented by the prosecution — it was overwhelming.
mnemonic: GAINSAY = GAIN + SAY — you SAY something AGAINST a claim to gain the upper hand.

definition: To shock or excite someone into taking action; to stimulate or invigorate into sudden activity.
example: The coach's halftime speech galvanized the team into a stunning second-half comeback.
mnemonic: GALVANI (the scientist) shocked frog legs to life — galvanize = shock into action.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Excessively talkative, especially on trivial matters.
example: The garrulous salesman talked for an hour before the customer could get a word in.
mnemonic: GARR-ulous: a GARR-ling mouth that never stops — gargle of words.

definition: Lacking social grace or tact; awkward and unsophisticated in social situations.
example: Asking about the host's salary was considered gauche even by the most lenient social standards.
mnemonic: GAUCHE means 'left' in French — socially, being 'left-footed' is clumsy and awkward.

definition: Awkward and ungainly in movement or appearance; lacking coordination or social grace.
example: The gawky teenager knocked over a display while trying to navigate the crowded store.
mnemonic: GAWK (to stare stupidly) + Y: so awkward others gawk at you.

definition: Relevant and appropriate to the matter at hand; pertinent.
example: The attorney objected that the witness's personal history was not germane to the case.
mnemonic: Germane = 'of the same germ' (Latin germanus: related) — closely related to the topic.

definition: Fluent and persuasive but insincere or superficial; too smooth to be trustworthy.
example: His glib answers during the debate masked a shallow grasp of the policy issues.
mnemonic: GLIB — slippery like lip gloss; smooth talk that slides right past the truth.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To dwell on one's own success or another's misfortune with smug, self-satisfied pleasure.
example: After winning the championship, the team captain was tempted to gloat over their rivals.
mnemonic: GLOAT: glow + float — you glow with smug pride and float above others.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Quietly dejected and sullen, often showing unhappiness through silence or a downcast manner.
example: After learning he had failed the exam, Marcus sat glum and silent in the corner.
mnemonic: Glum sounds like 'gloomy' — picture someone with a drooping, glum face.

definition: To provoke or irritate someone persistently into a reaction, often using taunts or annoyances.
example: The bully goaded his classmate with constant taunts until the classmate finally snapped.
mnemonic: A GOAD is a sharp stick used to prod animals — goad = jab until they move.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To give pleasure or satisfaction to; to please or indulge someone's desires or expectations.
example: It gratified the author to learn that her book had changed a reader's life.
mnemonic: Gratify = grat (grateful/pleasing) — to make someone feel pleased.

definition: Uncalled for; lacking any good reason; done without necessity or justification.
example: Critics objected to the gratuitous violence in the film, arguing it served no narrative purpose.
mnemonic: GRATUITOUS = GRATUITY (tip) given for no work — something given or done with no good reason.
confusableNote: Gratuitous can also mean 'free of charge' in older usage; on the GRE it means 'unjustified/unnecessary.'

definition: Fond of the company of others; sociable and outgoing by nature.
example: His gregarious personality made him a natural host who put every guest at ease.
mnemonic: GREGAR-ious: Latin grex = flock — someone who loves being in the flock with others.

definition: A real or perceived cause of distress or resentment, especially one that warrants a formal complaint.
example: The workers filed a formal grievance with management over the unsafe working conditions.
mnemonic: 'Grieve' + '-ance': something that causes you to grieve or feel wronged.

definition: To act in an obsequiously servile manner; to humble oneself excessively before another.
example: Rather than grovel before his critics, the artist stood by his controversial work.
mnemonic: GROVEL: picture crawling on the GRAVEL at someone's feet, begging.

definition: To complain in a low, discontented voice; to mutter resentfully or with irritation.
example: The workers grumbled about the new overtime policy but did not formally protest.
mnemonic: GRUMBLE sounds like a stomach rumbling with discontent — low and ongoing.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Easily deceived or cheated due to excessive trust and naivety.
example: The gullible investor handed over his savings after a single phone call from the fraudster.
mnemonic: GULL — a seabird easily lured; to 'gull' someone means to trick them.

definition: Made commonplace or stale through overuse; lacking freshness or originality.
example: The hackneyed metaphors in her essay revealed a writer who had not yet developed her own voice.
mnemonic: HACKNEY horses were overworked hired horses — hackneyed phrases are overworked and worn out.

definition: To hinder or obstruct the movement or progress of someone or something.
example: Heavy fog hampered the rescue operation for most of the morning.
mnemonic: A HAMPER is a basket that traps laundry — hamper = trap and slow things down.
confusableNote: hamper (noun): on the GRE, hamper as a verb means to hinder, not a laundry basket — a common trap.

definition: Lacking any order, plan, or direction; occurring purely by chance or accident.
example: Books were piled in haphazard stacks with no regard for subject, author, or size.
mnemonic: HAP (luck) + HAZARD (chance) — double dose of randomness.

definition: Unfortunate and deserving of pity; persistently unlucky and ineffectual.
example: The hapless candidate lost three elections despite running tireless campaigns.
mnemonic: HAP means luck; HAPless = without luck.

definition: To lecture or scold someone at length in an aggressive, domineering manner.
example: The supervisor harangued the team for an hour about missed deadlines.
mnemonic: HA + RANG: rang like a loud bell — a prolonged, clanging verbal assault.

definition: A person or thing that signals or foreshadows what is to come.
example: The first robin of spring is a beloved harbinger of warmer weather.
mnemonic: A HARBINGER finds the HARBOR first — a scout announcing what arrives next.

definition: Intensely distressing or traumatic, causing acute mental anguish.
example: Survivors gave harrowing accounts of the earthquake's destruction and the desperate search for loved ones.
mnemonic: HARROW (a farm tool that tears soil) — tears through your emotions like a harrow through earth.

definition: Arrogantly superior and disdainful of others, as if looking down from great height.
example: The haughty duchess refused to acknowledge the servants when she swept through the hall.
mnemonic: HAUGHTY sounds like 'haute' (high French fashion) — someone who thinks they're above everyone.

definition: Stubbornly determined to have one's own way; refusing to be controlled or directed.
example: The headstrong general ignored his advisors and launched the ill-advised offensive alone.
mnemonic: HEAD is STRONG — the head dominates, refusing any outside steering.

definition: Producing a powerful, exhilarating, or intoxicating effect on the emotions or senses.
example: The heady rush of her first solo performance left the young violinist trembling with excitement.
mnemonic: HEADY — goes straight to your HEAD and makes you dizzy with intense feeling.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To pay careful attention to advice or a warning; to take notice and act accordingly.
example: Had he heeded the weather report, he would never have set out on the voyage.
mnemonic: HEED sounds like 'head' — use your head, listen up, and pay attention.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Departing from accepted or established doctrines or beliefs; unorthodox in opinion.
example: Her heterodox interpretation of classical texts unsettled the traditionalists in the department.
mnemonic: HETERO (different) + DOX (belief) — holding a different belief from the mainstream.
confusableNote: Contrast with 'orthodox' (conforming to established doctrine); 'heterodox' is its opposite.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Composed of elements of different kinds; diverse and varied in composition.
example: The city's heterogeneous population made it a rich cultural mosaic of languages and traditions.
mnemonic: HETEROGENEOUS: 'hetero' (different) + 'genus' (kind) — many different kinds mixed together.
confusableNote: Directly contrasts with 'homogeneous' (all the same kind).

definition: Excessively narrow-minded and resistant to change due to rigid adherence to tradition.
example: The hidebound trustees refused to modernize the museum's collection policies.
mnemonic: A hide-bound animal is starved and stiff — hidebound thinkers are rigid and stuck.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To make it difficult for someone to do something or for something to happen.
example: Lack of funding continues to hinder scientific progress in the region.
mnemonic: HINDER = put something BEHIND you as an obstacle — block progress.

definition: Overly theatrical or melodramatic in behavior or expression; designed for effect rather than sincerity.
example: Her histrionic outburst at the meeting was so exaggerated that no one took her grievances seriously.
mnemonic: HISTRIONIC shares 'histrio' (Latin actor) — drama queen on stage.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'historical'; histrionic relates to acting, not history.

definition: A confused, disorganized mixture of diverse and unrelated elements; a jumble.
example: The curriculum was a hodgepodge of outdated textbooks, online modules, and borrowed worksheets.
mnemonic: HODGE-PODGE — toss everything into one pot and stir; you get a messy mix.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Of the same kind throughout; uniform in composition or character.
example: The homogeneous community had shared the same customs and language for generations without outside influence.
mnemonic: HOMOGENEOUS: 'homo' (same) + 'genus' (kind) — all one kind, like homogenized milk.
confusableNote: Directly contrasts with 'heterogeneous' (mixed, diverse kinds).

definition: Lacking variety or excitement; tediously ordinary and monotonous.
example: She yearned for adventure to break the humdrum routine of her suburban life.
mnemonic: 'Hum' + 'drum' — the dull, repetitive hum of a drum beaten the same way endlessly.

definition: Deliberate and obvious exaggeration used for emphasis or rhetorical effect, not meant literally.
example: Saying 'I've told you a million times' is a classic hyperbole, not a literal count.
mnemonic: HYPER-BOLE — hyper means over the top; hyperbole goes way over the top.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'hyperbola' (a mathematical curve); hyperbole is a figure of speech.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: A person who claims moral virtues or beliefs they do not actually hold or practice.
example: The senator was exposed as a hypocrite when his private behavior contradicted his public crusade.
mnemonic: HYPO (under) + CRITE (critic) — secretly beneath the moral standard they publicly judge others by.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Marked by uncontrollable, overwhelming emotion; wildly unrestrained in feeling or behavior.
example: The audience became hysterical when the fire alarm sounded mid-performance, causing a stampede.
mnemonic: HYSTERIA — like 'history' of emotional explosions; your emotions go off the charts.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Characterized by attacking or rejecting cherished beliefs, institutions, or conventional ideas.
example: His iconoclastic approach to economics challenged assumptions that had stood for over a century.
mnemonic: ICON (sacred image) + CLAST (breaker) — an iconoclast smashes sacred idols and conventions.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: A person who rigidly adheres to an ideology, often prioritizing doctrine over practical considerations.
example: The ideologue refused to compromise on any point, even when pragmatic adjustments would have saved the bill.
mnemonic: Ideo (idea) + logue (speaker) — someone who only speaks in the language of their fixed ideas.

definition: Peculiar or distinctive to an individual; characteristic in a quirky or unusual personal way.
example: Her idiosyncratic habit of underlining every third word made her notes puzzling to others.
mnemonic: IDIO (personal/own) — idiosyncratic traits are your own uniquely odd private patterns.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Based on illusion; not real but deceptively appearing to be true or valid.
example: The economic boom proved illusory, collapsing within months under the weight of debt.
mnemonic: ILLUSORY = ILLUSION + ORY — full of illusions, nothing solid underneath.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'illusionary,' a synonym, or 'illusive,' which is slightly narrower in meaning.

definition: A complicated, confusing, and embarrassing entanglement or political scandal.
example: The senator resigned amid an imbroglio involving misuse of campaign funds.
mnemonic: IM-BROGLIO — sounds like 'I'm in broth,' deeply tangled and stewing.

definition: Perfectly clean, flawless, or morally without any fault or blemish.
example: Her immaculate record of public service made her an ideal candidate for the judiciary.
mnemonic: IM- (not) + MACULA (Latin: spot) = without a spot or stain.

definition: About to happen very soon; impending.
example: With the hurricane imminent, residents were ordered to evacuate the coast.
mnemonic: IMMINENT — think 'I'm in it soon'; it's right on top of you.
confusableNote: imminent vs. eminent: imminent = about to happen; eminent = distinguished or prominent.

definition: To enclose or imprison within walls; to confine or restrict so as to isolate.
example: The political dissident was immured in a remote prison for years without trial.
mnemonic: IM + MURE (wall) — immure = put in behind a wall (mural = wall painting).

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Not subject to change; fixed, permanent, and unalterable in nature or character.
example: The laws of thermodynamics are immutable; no experiment has ever violated them.
mnemonic: IM (not) + MUTABLE (changeable) — immutable = cannot be mutated or changed.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To weaken or damage the strength, quality, or functioning of something.
example: Alcohol impairs judgment and significantly slows reaction time.
mnemonic: IM + PAIR — un-pairs proper function, breaking the paired system.

definition: To delay or obstruct progress by interfering with someone's movement or actions.
example: Bureaucratic red tape impeded the delivery of emergency aid to the affected region.
mnemonic: IM + PEDE (foot) — put a foot in the way to block movement.

definition: A circumstance or obstacle that makes progress or achievement difficult.
example: A lack of transportation remained the greatest impediment to accessing healthcare in rural areas.
mnemonic: IMPEDIMENT = the noun form of IMPEDE — the thing that blocks your feet.

definition: Impossible to understand or comprehend; too dense or obscure to penetrate mentally.
example: The legal contract was so impenetrable that even lawyers required hours to interpret its clauses.
mnemonic: IMPENETRABLE = IM (not) + PENETRATE — your mind simply cannot get through.

definition: Absolutely necessary or urgently required; admitting of no delay or avoidance.
example: It is imperative that all personnel evacuate the building immediately when the alarm sounds.
mnemonic: IMPERATIVE = EMPEROR's order — an emperor's command that cannot be refused.

definition: Assuming power or authority without justification; arrogantly domineering and commanding.
example: The imperious manager barked orders without explanation, expecting instant obedience from the staff.
mnemonic: IMPERIOUS = like an emperor — commanding everyone as if by imperial decree.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'imperial' (of an empire); imperious describes arrogant behavior, not political authority.

definition: Not showing proper respect; rudely bold or presumptuous toward those of higher authority.
example: The student's impertinent response to the professor's criticism shocked the entire class.
mnemonic: IM- (not) + PERTINENT: not sticking to what's pertinent — overstepping your place.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'irrelevant' (not pertinent to a topic); impertinent means disrespectfully rude.

definition: Unable to be upset or excited; remaining calm and composed under any circumstances.
example: The surgeon remained imperturbable even as unexpected complications arose mid-operation.
mnemonic: 'Im-' (not) + 'perturb' (disturb): nothing disturbs this person.

definition: Not penetrable or affected by something; wholly unresponsive to influence or harm.
example: Raised in a stoic household, she seemed impervious to criticism, no matter how harsh.
mnemonic: IM (not) + PERVIOUS (allowing passage) — nothing passes through; completely sealed off.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'imperious' (arrogantly domineering); 'impervious' means unaffected, not bossy.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Acting suddenly with great force or without careful thought; rashly hasty and unpredictable.
example: His impetuous decision to quit his job without a backup plan shocked his family.
mnemonic: IMPETUOUS: im-PET-uous — like a pet who bolts out the door the moment it opens.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Impossible to appease, pacify, or persuade; relentlessly uncompromising.
example: The implacable prosecutor refused every plea bargain, insisting on a full trial.
mnemonic: IM- (not) + PLACATE: cannot be placated — impossible to calm down.
confusableNote: implacable vs. inexorable: both mean unyielding, but inexorable stresses inevitability; implacable stresses inability to be appeased.

definition: Implied or understood without being directly stated; not explicitly expressed.
example: There was an implicit agreement among the partners that no one would speak to the press.
mnemonic: IM-PLICIT: 'plicit' like 'explicit' — implicit is the hidden layer beneath what is explicit.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'explicit' (clearly stated); 'implicit' is the unstated, hidden meaning.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Unwise or tactless, especially in a way that is likely to cause offense or disadvantage.
example: It was impolitic to criticize the donor's pet project during the fundraising dinner.
mnemonic: IM- (not) + POLITIC (shrewd/tactful) = not tactful or diplomatically wise.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'impolite' (rude); impolitic means strategically unwise or ill-judged.

definition: To create or perform something spontaneously without prior preparation, using available resources.
example: When the projector failed, the lecturer improvised with hand-drawn diagrams on the chalkboard.
mnemonic: IM-PROVISE: without (im) prior vision — making it up on the spot without foresight.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Boldly disrespectful and shameless in manner or speech; brazenly insolent.
example: The impudent clerk laughed openly at the manager's directive.
mnemonic: IM- (not) + PUDENT (from Latin pudere: to be ashamed) = shameless and disrespectful.

definition: To dispute the truth, validity, or honesty of something; to call into question.
example: Defense counsel sought to impugn the credibility of the star witness through cross-examination.
mnemonic: IMPUGN = IM-PUGN — like punching (pugnacious) a claim to knock it down.

definition: Acting on sudden urges or desires without deliberation; driven by impulse rather than reason.
example: Her impulsive purchase of a boat made no sense given that she lived far from any water.
mnemonic: IMPULSE + ive — you follow every impulse that fires without pausing to think.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Impossible to be taken away or transferred; denoting rights that cannot be surrendered or removed.
example: The declaration proclaimed life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness to be inalienable rights.
mnemonic: In- (not) + alienable (transferable) = cannot be alienated or taken away from someone.

definition: Existing from birth; naturally inherent rather than acquired through experience or learning.
example: Researchers debate whether mathematical intuition is inborn or developed through early education.
mnemonic: IN + BORN — it was in you the moment you were born, no learning required.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Tending to inflame passion, controversy, or hostility; deliberately provocative and inflammatory.
example: The politician's incendiary remarks about immigrants sparked protests outside the convention hall.
mnemonic: INCENDIARY: incendio (fire in Spanish/Italian) — sets emotional fires wherever it goes.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Continuing without interruption; never ceasing.
example: The incessant noise from the construction site made concentration impossible.
mnemonic: IN + CESSANT (cease) — in-capable of ceasing; never stops.

definition: Just begun and not fully developed or organized; rudimentary and incomplete.
example: Her inchoate ideas about social reform had yet to coalesce into a coherent platform.
mnemonic: IN-CHO-ate: 'in' (not yet) + 'choate' (complete) — an inchoate plan is not yet whole.

definition: Occurring as a minor accompaniment to something else; not of primary importance.
example: The cost savings were incidental benefits of the policy, not its primary motivation.
mnemonic: INCIDENTAL: an INCIDENT on the side — happens alongside the main event, not central to it.

definition: To stir up or urge someone to act, especially in a violent or harmful way.
example: The fiery speech was designed to incite the crowd to march on the government building.
mnemonic: IN-CITE: 'cite' from Latin citare (to set in motion) — set someone in motion.

definition: Having a natural tendency, preference, or disposition toward something.
example: She was inclined to trust her instincts rather than rely solely on data.
mnemonic: inclined like a hill — you naturally lean or slope toward one side.

definition: Not fitting together harmoniously; out of place or inconsistent with the surroundings.
example: The ultramodern skyscraper looked incongruous amid the historic cobblestone district.
mnemonic: In- (not) + congruere (to agree) — things that simply don't agree or fit together.

definition: Not able to be denied or disputed; indisputably true and beyond challenge.
example: The fingerprints provided incontrovertible proof that the defendant had been at the crime scene.
mnemonic: INCONTROVERTIBLE = IN (not) + CONTROVERT (dispute) — impossible to argue against.

definition: Unwilling or unable to believe something; showing disbelief or skepticism.
example: The scientist was incredulous when she first heard the claim that the specimen had regenerated.
mnemonic: IN (not) + CREDULOUS (believing) — not credulous at all; you can't swallow that story.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'incredible' (unbelievable); 'incredulous' describes the doubter, not the claim.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Not in keeping with accepted standards of good taste or decency; improper in behavior.
example: Laughing loudly at a funeral was considered indecorous by every guest present.
mnemonic: IN- + DECOROUS (proper) = not decorated with good manners.

definition: Incapable of being tired out; persisting tirelessly despite prolonged effort.
example: The indefatigable researcher worked eighteen-hour days for a decade to complete her study.
mnemonic: IN- (not) + DEFATIGABLE (tire out): cannot be fatigued, ever.

definition: Not clearly defined, fixed, or determined; vague, ambiguous, or without clear boundaries.
example: The treaty contained several indefinite clauses that left both parties uncertain of their obligations.
mnemonic: IN (not) + DEFINITE — without a clear definition or boundary; fuzzy and unresolved.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: A formal legal charge or accusation of a serious crime; also, a strong condemnation of something.
example: The grand jury handed down an indictment on charges of fraud and obstruction of justice.
mnemonic: Indict = 'in-dict' — words spoken (dict) against (in) someone, formally charging them.
confusableNote: Note the silent 'c' in indictment — pronounced 'in-DITE-ment,' not 'in-DICT-ment.'

definition: Feeling or showing anger aroused by something perceived as unjust or unworthy.
example: She was indignant when her original idea was presented by a colleague as his own.
mnemonic: 'Indignant' shares root with 'dignity' — angry because one's dignity was insulted.

definition: Not following a straight or obvious course; avoiding explicit statement by going around an issue.
example: Rather than criticizing the plan outright, he raised indirect objections through carefully worded questions.
mnemonic: IN-DIRECT — the opposite of direct: taking the long way around.

definition: Done without careful judgment or distinction; applying to all cases without selection.
example: Indiscriminate logging destroyed habitat for dozens of species that could have been spared.
mnemonic: IN-DISCRIMINATE — failing to discriminate (distinguish) between things; treating everything the same.

definition: Absolutely necessary; impossible to do without.
example: Clean water is indispensable to all known forms of life on Earth.
mnemonic: IN + DISPENSABLE — cannot be dispensed with, cannot be set aside.

definition: Habitually lazy and averse to activity or exertion; showing a dislike of work.
example: The indolent student waited until the night before the deadline to begin his research paper.
mnemonic: INDOLENT: in-DOLE-nt — collecting the dole (welfare) rather than working; lazy by habit.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To bring about or cause something to happen; to persuade someone to do something.
example: The promise of tax credits induced many companies to invest in renewable energy.
mnemonic: IN-DUCE: 'duce' from Latin ducere (to lead) — lead someone into doing something.

definition: Habitually diligent and hardworking; characterized by steady, earnest effort.
example: The industrious apprentice arrived first each morning and left long after her colleagues.
mnemonic: INDUSTRIAL — factories are always busy; an industrious person is a human factory.

definition: Impossible to avoid, resist, or escape; inescapable by any means.
example: Death is the one ineluctable fact that every philosophical tradition must ultimately confront.
mnemonic: IN (not) + ELUCTABLE (able to wrestle free) — you cannot wrestle free from it.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Having or showing no skill; incompetent and clumsy in action.
example: The inept carpenter's crooked shelves collapsed under the weight of a few books.
mnemonic: IN + APT — not apt (not fit); entirely unsuited to the task.
confusableNote: inept vs. inapt — inept stresses incompetence; inapt simply means not appropriate or suitable.

definition: An inapt or clumsy expression; an inappropriate or unfortunate choice of words.
example: The speech was marred by several infelicities that left the audience puzzled rather than moved.
mnemonic: IN-FELICITY: in (not) + felicity (happiness/aptness) — a word choice that just doesn't fit right.
confusableNote: Contrast with 'felicity' (apt and pleasing expression); 'infelicity' is its clumsy opposite.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Physically weak or frail, especially as a result of old age or illness.
example: The infirm patient required daily assistance with even basic household tasks.
mnemonic: IN- (not) + FIRM: not firm or strong in body — weakened and unsteady.
confusableNote: infirm vs. infirmary: an infirmary is a place that treats the infirm (the physically weak).

definition: To give shape, character, or essential quality to something; to animate or influence fundamentally.
example: A deep commitment to social justice informed every policy decision the minister made throughout her career.
mnemonic: Inform = put form INTO something — it gives shape to ideas and decisions from within.
confusableNote: The GRE sense ('to shape or give character to') is not the everyday sense of 'to tell someone something.'

definition: Remarkably clever, original, and inventive in design or solution.
example: The engineer devised an ingenious pulley system that allowed one person to lift enormous loads.
mnemonic: INGENIOUS — like a genius; born of ingenuity and creative intelligence.
confusableNote: ingenious vs. ingenuous — ingenious means cleverly inventive; ingenuous means innocent and candid.

definition: Innocent, candid, and unsuspecting; naively trusting and free from guile or deceit.
example: Her ingenuous belief that everyone told the truth made her an easy target for con artists.
mnemonic: INGENUOUS vs. INGENIOUS — ingenuous is open and innocent; notice the u (un-guarded).
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'ingenious' (cleverly inventive); 'ingenuous' means innocent and naive.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Deeply embedded in one's nature or habits; firmly established over time.
example: His ingrained distrust of authority made him question every rule he encountered.
mnemonic: IN the GRAIN of wood — a pattern running so deep it can't be sanded away.

definition: To restrain, hinder, or suppress an action, process, or impulse.
example: Fear of failure often inhibits people from attempting creative endeavors.
mnemonic: IN + HIBIT (have) — to hold back from having or doing something.
confusableNote: prohibit: prohibit means to formally forbid by rule; inhibit means to restrain or hold back, often internally.

definition: Actively hostile to or harmful toward a person, cause, or principle.
example: Policies inimical to free expression were swiftly challenged in the courts.
mnemonic: IN + ENEMY + cal — like the word 'enemy' lives inside it; inherently enemy-like.

definition: Not harmful or offensive in any way; having no adverse effect whatsoever.
example: What seemed like an innocuous remark about the weather somehow offended the entire table.
mnemonic: IN (not) + NOCUOUS (harmful from 'nocere') — nocuous = harmful, so innocuous = not harmful.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Exceeding reasonable limits; disproportionately large or excessive.
example: He spent an inordinate amount of time on the introduction, leaving little for the body of the argument.
mnemonic: INORDINATE: IN- + ordinate (ordered) — out of order, way beyond what's appropriate.

definition: Eager to learn and know things; having a strong desire to investigate and inquire.
example: The inquisitive child peppered the museum guide with questions about every exhibit they passed.
mnemonic: INQUIRE + itive — always inquiring, always asking 'why?'
confusableNote: inquisitive vs. nosy — inquisitive implies genuine intellectual curiosity; nosy implies intrusive prying into others' affairs.

definition: Impossible to understand or interpret; giving no clues to one's thoughts or meaning.
example: The judge's inscrutable expression gave the attorneys no hint of how she would rule.
mnemonic: INSCRUTABLE = IN (not) + SCRUTE (scrutinize) — can't examine or read it at all.

definition: Gradually and subtly causing harm or danger while appearing harmless; treacherously deceptive.
example: The insidious effects of misinformation spread quietly through the population before anyone noticed.
mnemonic: INSIDIOUS: inside + devious — hidden inside, deviously spreading harm before you detect it.
confusableNote: Contrast with 'flagrant' (openly harmful); insidious harm is hidden and gradual.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Lacking vigor, interest, or distinctiveness; flat and flavorless in quality.
example: The insipid dialogue made it impossible to care about any of the characters in the play.
mnemonic: IN + SAPID (tasty): without taste or interest — flavorless in every sense.

definition: Showing a rude and arrogant lack of respect toward authority or others.
example: The insolent teenager rolled her eyes at every question the teacher asked.
mnemonic: IN- + SOL (sun): someone so 'in the sun' of their own pride they disrespect everyone else.

definition: To bring about or initiate an action or event, often something negative.
example: Investigators determined that a single agitator had instigated the violent confrontation.
mnemonic: IN-STIG-ate: 'stig' from Latin instigare (to goad) — prod or poke someone into action.

definition: Ignorant of or indifferent to cultures or ideas outside one's own narrow experience; isolated.
example: The insular community resisted all outside influences, including modern technology.
mnemonic: INSUL-ar: from Latin insula (island) — cut off from the mainland, like an island.

definition: Able to be understood; expressed clearly enough to be comprehended.
example: The translator's goal was to produce an intelligible version of the ancient text for modern readers.
mnemonic: INTELLIGIBLE = INTELLECT + IBLE — readable by the intellect, clear to the mind.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'intelligent,' which describes mental capacity rather than clarity of expression.

definition: Able to be used in place of each other without any difference in function or effect.
example: The two technical terms are interchangeable in most scientific literature.
mnemonic: Inter- (between) + change — you can change them between each other freely.

definition: A person who acts as a mediator or go-between connecting two parties in a negotiation or transaction.
example: The diplomat served as an intermediary between the warring factions, shuttling proposals back and forth.
mnemonic: Intermediary = 'inter' (between) + 'mediary' — someone who stands in the middle between two sides.

definition: Seemingly endless; tediously long without any apparent end.
example: The interminable board meeting stretched past midnight with no resolution in sight.
mnemonic: IN + TERMINAL — no terminal (ending) in sight; goes on forever.

definition: Occurring at irregular intervals; not continuous or steady.
example: Intermittent rain showers forced the outdoor concert to pause several times.
mnemonic: INTER (between) + MITTENT (sending) — sending signals between gaps, not constantly.

definition: Twisted or woven closely together; closely connected and difficult to separate.
example: The fates of the two nations were so intertwined that neither could act independently.
mnemonic: Inter- (between) + twined — threads twisted between each other, inseparable.

definition: To hint or suggest something indirectly without stating it explicitly.
example: The chairman's careful phrasing intimated that layoffs were coming, though no announcement was made.
mnemonic: Intimate (verb) = whisper into someone's ear — hinting privately rather than declaring openly.
confusableNote: The GRE tests 'intimate' as a verb meaning to hint — not the familiar adjective meaning 'close' or 'personal.'

definition: Refusing to compromise or abandon an extreme position; uncompromisingly stubborn.
example: The intransigent negotiator torpedoed the peace talks by rejecting every proposed concession.
mnemonic: IN- (not) + TRANSIGENT (from Latin transigere, to reach agreement): no deal possible.

definition: Fearless and adventurous; characterized by resolute courage in the face of danger.
example: The intrepid journalist reported from the front lines despite constant artillery fire.
mnemonic: IN- (not) + TREPID (frightened): not trembling — bold as can be.
confusableNote: intrepid vs. trepid: trepid means fearful; intrepid is its exact opposite.

definition: A secret scheme or plot; also the fascination aroused by something mysterious or complex.
example: The documentary's intrigue lay in the never-before-seen footage of the reclusive artist.
mnemonic: INTRIGUE: in-TRIG-ue — a hidden trigger (trig) set to go off; secret and captivating.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Belonging naturally and essentially to something; inherent rather than external or acquired.
example: Philosophers debate whether moral value is intrinsic to human beings or assigned by social convention.
mnemonic: INTRINSIC = INSIDE + TRINKET — the value is inside, built in, not added on.
confusableNote: Contrasts with 'extrinsic' (originating from outside); intrinsic value is internal and independent of circumstance.

definition: Known or perceived through instinct rather than conscious reasoning or analysis.
example: Her intuitive grasp of social dynamics made her an exceptionally effective negotiator.
mnemonic: Intuitive = 'in-tuition': knowledge paid for inside, not by study.

definition: To overwhelm with an excessive amount; to flood or submerge completely.
example: After the product launch, the company was inundated with customer complaints.
mnemonic: IN-UND-ate: 'und' from Latin unda (wave) — waves crashing in, flooding everything.

definition: Tending to intrude on or infringe upon something; spreading into or encroaching where unwelcome.
example: The invasive species of vine overtook the garden beds within a single growing season.
mnemonic: INVADE + ive — invasive things invade your space without permission.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To persuade someone to do something by means of deception or flattery.
example: The lobbyist inveigled the legislator into supporting the bill by lavishing him with gifts.
mnemonic: INVEIGLE sounds like 'in veil' — veiling your true motive while coaxing someone along.

definition: Long-established in a habit or activity; deeply rooted and unlikely to change.
example: An inveterate traveler, she had visited every continent before her fortieth birthday.
mnemonic: INVETERATE = IN + VETERAN: a veteran of a habit, deeply entrenched over years.

definition: Likely to arouse resentment or envy because of perceived unfairness or offensive discrimination.
example: Making invidious comparisons between employees in a team meeting destroys morale.
mnemonic: INVIDIOUS: in-ENVY-ous — rooted in envy; it provokes envy and resentment in others.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'insidious' (secretly harmful); invidious provokes unfair resentment openly.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To give strength, energy, or vitality to; to animate and stimulate.
example: A brisk morning run invigorated her and sharpened her focus for the day ahead.
mnemonic: IN-VIGOR-ate: put 'vigor' (strength/energy) into something.

definition: Free from violation, injury, or desecration; kept sacred and intact.
example: The ancient treaty was considered inviolate, and no ruler dared to break its terms.
mnemonic: IN- (not) + VIOLATE = something that has not been and cannot be violated.

definition: Having a hot temper; prone to outbursts of anger with little provocation.
example: The irascible professor snapped at students for the slightest interruption.
mnemonic: 'Ira' is Latin for anger — an irascible person is always full of 'ira.'

definition: Intense anger or wrath, especially as provoked by an injustice.
example: The senator's remarks drew the ire of constituents across the political spectrum.
mnemonic: IRE: short and sharp like the hot flash of anger — one syllable, all rage.

definition: Causing irritation or annoyance; tediously troublesome.
example: The constant background noise from the construction site was irksome to every resident.
mnemonic: 'Irk' means to annoy — irksome is the adjective form, amplified.

definition: So strong or certain as to be impossible to weaken, dispute, or break.
example: The prosecution presented an ironclad alibi that completely cleared the suspect of any wrongdoing.
mnemonic: IRONCLAD = clad in IRON armor — impervious to any attack or challenge.

definition: Unable to make decisions; showing uncertainty and lack of firm purpose.
example: His irresolute leadership during the crisis left the team confused about next steps.
mnemonic: ir- (not) + resolute — not resolved, still spinning without landing.

definition: Showing a lack of respect for people or things that are generally held in high regard.
example: His irreverent comedy routines mocked religious ceremonies and political leaders alike.
mnemonic: IR- (not) + REVERENT (respectful) = not treating sacred things with reverence.

definition: Traveling from place to place, typically for work; not settled in one location.
example: The itinerant musician performed in a different city every week throughout the summer.
mnemonic: ITINERANT = ITINERARY + -ant: someone who always has a new itinerary, never settling.

definition: To put something at risk of loss, harm, or failure.
example: Leaking the classified documents could jeopardize the entire undercover operation.
mnemonic: JEOPARDY — like the game show where you risk everything on each answer.

definition: To discard or abandon something no longer wanted or useful.
example: The company jettisoned its unprofitable divisions to focus on core operations.
mnemonic: JET + TISON — jets throw cargo overboard in emergencies; jettison = throw out.

definition: Cheerful and light-hearted; marked by high spirits and a merry disposition.
example: The jocund crowd danced and sang throughout the harvest festival.
mnemonic: 'Jocund' echoes 'jocular' and 'joyful' — all words for cheerful good humor.
confusableNote: jocund vs. jocular: jocund = cheerfully happy; jocular = given to joking.

definition: Having or showing sound judgment and wisdom, especially in practical affairs.
example: A judicious allocation of resources allowed the nonprofit to serve three times as many clients.
mnemonic: JUDICIOUS = JUDGE + ICIOUS — like a fair, wise judge making the right call.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'judicial,' which pertains to courts of law, not personal wisdom.

definition: Having a sharp, penetrating, and eager intellect or perception.
example: A keen observer of human behavior, she noticed subtle inconsistencies others overlooked.
mnemonic: A keen blade cuts deep — a keen mind cuts through confusion just as sharply.

definition: Using very few words; brief and to the point in speech or expression.
example: His laconic reply — simply 'No' — ended the negotiation more effectively than a long argument would have.
mnemonic: LACONIC: from Laconia (Sparta) — Spartans were famous for brief, blunt speech.

definition: To criticize someone harshly and at length; to beat or attack verbally.
example: The opposition lambasted the budget proposal as reckless and fiscally irresponsible.
mnemonic: LAMB + BASTE: even a gentle lamb gets basted (beaten) — a forceful, thorough thrashing in words.

definition: To express sorrow, mourning, or regret for something lost or unfortunate.
example: The poet lamented the passing of an era when craftsmanship was prized above speed.
mnemonic: 'Lament' — the 'la' sounds like a mournful cry; imagine a wailing voice.

definition: To publicly mock or satirize someone or something in a sharp, often humorous way.
example: The sketch comedy show lampooned the politician's mannerisms to great audience laughter.
mnemonic: LAMP + OON: shine a bright lamp on someone's flaws for all to ridicule.

definition: Lacking energy or enthusiasm; slow-moving and weak in a way that suggests fatigue or indifference.
example: In the oppressive summer heat, even the most energetic children grew languid by noon.
mnemonic: 'Languid' contains 'languish' — picture someone languishing on a couch.
confusableNote: languid vs. limpid: languid = lacking energy; limpid = clear and transparent.

definition: To grow weak or feeble; to exist in a state of prolonged suffering or neglect.
example: The reform bill languished in committee for two years before anyone voted on it.
mnemonic: LANGUISH rhymes with ANGUISH — suffering and weakening over time.

definition: Generous and liberal giving of money or gifts, often by someone of wealth or power.
example: The philanthropist's largesse funded new wings at both the hospital and the art museum.
mnemonic: LARGE + sse — large-scale giving, grand in size and spirit.

definition: Physical or mental weariness; a state of listless fatigue and lack of energy.
example: After a month of overwork, a deep lassitude settled over her and she could barely rise each morning.
mnemonic: 'Lassitude' — think of being 'lax' (limp, slack): lax + itude = extreme slackness.

definition: Freedom from strict rules or limits; scope for independent action or judgment.
example: The new manager gave her team considerable latitude in choosing their own workflows.
mnemonic: LATI-tude: 'let it' be — permission to let things be as you choose.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'longitude'; both are geographic terms, but latitude here means figurative freedom from constraints.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Deserving high praise and commendation; praiseworthy in purpose or execution.
example: The charity's laudable mission attracted volunteers from every corner of the city.
mnemonic: Laud = praise; laudable = worthy of being lauded.

definition: Sumptuously rich, elaborate, or extravagant, often to an excessive degree.
example: The lavish banquet featured gold-plated tableware and a ten-course meal for three hundred guests.
mnemonic: LAVISH sounds like 'lav-ish' — washing in wealth, pouring it on.

definition: Not sufficiently strict, careful, or firm; negligent in enforcing standards.
example: The school's lax attendance policy led to widespread truancy among students.
mnemonic: LAX airport is relaxed — bags slip through security without strict checks.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Affected by lethargy; sluggish, drowsy, and lacking energy or motivation.
example: Recovering from illness, he spent the day lethargic in bed, unable to concentrate on anything.
mnemonic: From 'Lethe,' the river of forgetfulness in Greek myth — drinking from it made you forget and doze.

definition: Inappropriate lightness of manner or humor, especially in serious situations.
example: His levity during the memorial service struck the mourners as deeply disrespectful.
mnemonic: LEVity = LEVitate — treating heavy matters as if they float away weightlessly.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: A person or thing that is a disadvantage or handicap; a source of weakness.
example: The candidate's controversial past became a liability that no spin doctor could neutralize.
mnemonic: LIABLE = responsible for a problem; LIABILITY = being the problem itself.
confusableNote: liability vs. asset: they are antonyms — a liability weakens while an asset strengthens.

definition: A person who freely indulges in sensual pleasures with no regard for moral restraint.
example: The notorious libertine spent his fortune on dissolute pleasures and scandalous affairs.
mnemonic: A libertine takes 'liberty' too far — freedom from all moral limits.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Completely clear and easy to understand; transparent and unclouded in expression.
example: Her limpid prose made even the most difficult philosophical concepts seem effortlessly clear.
mnemonic: LIMPID = like a LIMPING stream — slow, clear, and transparent all the way to the bottom.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'limp,' meaning weak or flaccid; 'limpid' means crystal-clear.

definition: To treat someone as a celebrity or object of great admiration and public interest.
example: After winning the championship, the athlete was lionized by the entire city.
mnemonic: Lion = the king of beasts; to lionize = to treat someone like royalty.

definition: To feel intense disgust or hatred toward someone or something.
example: He loathed the hypocrisy of politicians who campaigned on values they privately abandoned.
mnemonic: LOATHE: sounds like 'load' of hatred — you're loaded with disgust.
confusableNote: loathe vs. loath: loathe (verb) = to hate intensely; loath (adjective) = reluctant or unwilling.

definition: Tending to talk a great deal; excessively fond of talking.
example: The loquacious host dominated every dinner conversation, leaving guests little chance to speak.
mnemonic: LOQU- (Latin: speak): LOQUACIOUS = speak + -acious (full of) — full of speaking.

definition: Expressed or presented in a clear, easily understood manner.
example: The professor's lucid explanation of quantum mechanics helped even beginners grasp the concept.
mnemonic: LUCid = LUCE (Latin: light) — shining light on ideas, making them clear.

definition: Producing a great deal of profit or financial gain.
example: She turned her hobby into a lucrative business, earning six figures within two years.
mnemonic: LUCR — from Latin 'lucrum' (gain); think 'lucre' = money.

definition: Excessively mournful or dismal, especially in a way that seems exaggerated or affected.
example: The actor delivered his lines in such a lugubrious tone that the comedy became unintentionally funny.
mnemonic: 'Lugubrious' — 'lugu' sounds like 'lugu-ber' (lugubris = mourning in Latin); picture a funeral.

definition: A temporary pause or calm period in an otherwise active or turbulent situation.
example: During the lull in fighting, aid workers rushed in supplies to the besieged city.
mnemonic: LULL: sounds like a lullaby — quiet, calm, things have slowed to sleep.

definition: Disturbing in a way that is associated with death or gruesome horror.
example: The artist's macabre paintings of skeletal figures unsettled gallery visitors.
mnemonic: MAC + ABRE: imagine a macabre scene where a MAC (knife) opens a grave — dark, grim.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Having or showing the authority or dignity of a master or magistrate; commanding and authoritative.
example: The professor delivered a magisterial lecture that left the audience in awe of her command of the subject.
mnemonic: MAGISTER (Latin: master/teacher) + IAL — possessing the manner of a grand master.

definition: Generous and forgiving, especially toward a rival or enemy; nobly free from pettiness.
example: The champion was magnanimous in victory, praising his opponent's skill and effort.
mnemonic: Magna (great) + animus (spirit) — a great spirit that rises above pettiness.

definition: Serving as a temporary substitute; hastily assembled from whatever is available.
example: Survivors built a makeshift shelter from fallen branches and scraps of cloth.
mnemonic: MAKE + SHIFT: you MAKE a SHIFT (change) quickly with whatever is on hand.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Having or showing a strong desire to harm others; deeply malicious.
example: The villain's malevolent grin made clear he took pleasure in others' suffering.
mnemonic: MALE (bad) + VOLENT (wishing) — wishing bad things upon others.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'malevolent' and 'maleficent'; malevolent stresses the wish to harm, maleficent the actual doing of harm.

definition: Wrongdoing or misconduct, especially by a public official in the exercise of official duties.
example: The inspector general's report detailed widespread malfeasance in the awarding of government contracts.
mnemonic: MAL (bad) + FEASANCE (doing) — bad doing by those in power.
confusableNote: Malfeasance (wrongful act) vs. misfeasance (lawful act done improperly) vs. nonfeasance (failure to act) — legal distinctions.

definition: To speak about someone in a spitefully critical and unfair manner; to slander.
example: Former allies maligned the general's reputation after the failed campaign.
mnemonic: MAL + IGN: mal = bad — to speak badly about someone, casting them in an evil light.
confusableNote: malign (v.) vs. malign (adj.): the adjective means harmful or evil in nature; the verb means to slander.

definition: Easily shaped, influenced, or adapted; capable of being molded by external forces.
example: Young minds are malleable, making early education critically important for character development.
mnemonic: MALLEABLE = MALLET + -able: can be shaped by a mallet — soft enough to be hammered into form.

definition: To restrain or confine with or as if with handcuffs; to shackle or fetter.
example: Rigid regulations manacled innovation, preventing the industry from adapting to new realities.
mnemonic: MANACLE = MAN +ACLE — a shackle that encircles the hand of a man.

definition: To display or make evident; to show a quality or feeling clearly by one's actions or appearance.
example: The new government manifested its commitment to reform by immediately releasing political prisoners.
mnemonic: Manifest = 'mani' (hand) + fest (festival) — show it openly, with your own hands, for all to see.
confusableNote: Manifest as an adjective means 'obvious'; as a noun it means a list of cargo — context determines sense.

definition: To treat a person or group as insignificant or peripheral, relegating them to powerless status.
example: The policy effectively marginalized rural communities by concentrating all funding in urban centers.
mnemonic: MARGINALIZE: push to the MARGIN — written off to the side where no one reads.

definition: A strict disciplinarian who insists on exact conformity to rules and orders.
example: The new principal was a martinet who enforced every regulation to the letter.
mnemonic: MARTINET — from General Martinet, a famously rigid French drill master.

definition: Excessively and weakly sentimental; sappy or sickeningly emotional.
example: Critics dismissed the film as mawkish, noting its manipulative use of tragic backstories.
mnemonic: 'Mawkish' sounds like 'mawk' (maggot) — something disgustingly sweet, making you queasy.
confusableNote: mawkish vs. maudlin: both mean overly sentimental, but maudlin often implies tearful drunkenness.

definition: Lacking in quantity or quality; deficient and inadequate.
example: The refugees survived on meager rations of rice and water for weeks before aid arrived.
mnemonic: MEAGER: sounds like 'meager meal' — barely enough to sustain you.

definition: To intervene between disputing parties to bring about agreement or reconciliation.
example: A neutral arbitrator was called in to mediate between the striking workers and management.
mnemonic: MEDI-ate: stand in the MIDDLE between two sides — be the mediator.

definition: The habit or practice of telling lies; untruthfulness.
example: The senator's mendacity was exposed when reporters uncovered documents contradicting his sworn testimony.
mnemonic: MENDacity — a liar MENDS the truth to suit their needs.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'mendicancy,' which means the practice of begging.

definition: Primarily motivated by personal financial gain rather than ethics or loyalty.
example: Critics accused the consultant of giving mercenary advice designed to maximize his fees, not the client's welfare.
mnemonic: MERCENARY — a soldier-for-hire; works only for money, not loyalty.

definition: Subject to sudden, unpredictable changes of mood or temperament; volatile and quicksilver.
example: His mercurial personality made him an exciting collaborator but an unreliable partner on long projects.
mnemonic: MERCURY (planet/god of speed) — quick to change, impossible to pin down.

definition: Deserving of praise, reward, or commendation on account of outstanding merit or service.
example: The soldier received a medal for meritorious conduct under extreme battlefield conditions.
mnemonic: Merit + orious — bursting with merit, worthy of a reward.

definition: Describing something using a comparison or metaphor rather than literal meaning.
example: Calling life a journey is metaphorical — we are not literally traveling a road.
mnemonic: META (beyond) + PHOR (carry): metaphorical carries meaning BEYOND the literal.

definition: Performed or behaving in a careful, systematic, orderly manner according to a plan.
example: The forensic investigator was methodical, examining every inch of the scene before drawing conclusions.
mnemonic: METHOD + -ical — following a METHOD step by step with discipline.

definition: Showing extreme care and precision in attention to detail; painstakingly accurate.
example: The meticulous archaeologist brushed each artifact for hours before cataloguing it.
mnemonic: From Latin METUS (fear) — originally fearful of making errors, now perfectly careful.

definition: Full of spirit and courage; spiritedly brave and eager to face challenges.
example: The mettlesome rookie firefighter rushed into the burning building without hesitation.
mnemonic: METTLE = courage; METTLESOME = brimming with mettle, like a spirited racehorse.
confusableNote: mettlesome vs. meddlesome: mettlesome means brave; meddlesome means intrusively interfering.

definition: To copy or imitate closely, especially the appearance or actions of another.
example: The comedian mimicked the president's distinctive vocal mannerisms to great effect.
mnemonic: MIMIC sounds like 'me-me' — a copycat always says 'me too, me too.'

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Extremely small in size or amount; tiny.
example: The forensic analyst found minute traces of the substance invisible to the naked eye.
mnemonic: MINUTE (adj): pronounced my-NEWT — shrunk down to a newt's scale, almost nothing.
confusableNote: As an adjective (my-NOOT), minute means tiny — distinct from the noun 'minute' (60 seconds).

definition: Characterized by a general hatred, distrust, or contempt for humankind.
example: His misanthropic worldview made genuine friendship nearly impossible to sustain.
mnemonic: MIS-ANTHROPIC: 'mis' (hate) + 'anthropo' (human) — hating all of humanity.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'misogynistic' (hatred of women specifically) or 'misogamistic' (hatred of marriage); misanthropic targets all people.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: A person who behaves badly or commits crimes; a villain or wrongdoer.
example: The sheriff rounded up the miscreants who had vandalized the town square.
mnemonic: MIS-CREANT: mis (wrong) + creant (believer) — someone who believes in doing wrong.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Reluctant to spend money; extremely stingy and unwilling to part with possessions.
example: The miserly executive refused to turn on the office heat even during the coldest winter months.
mnemonic: MISER + ly — like a miser, hoarding every coin in a dark room.

definition: A wrong or inaccurate name or designation applied to a person, place, or thing.
example: Calling the peanut a 'nut' is a misnomer, as it is botanically classified as a legume.
mnemonic: Mis (wrong) + nomer (name, as in 'nomenclature') = a wrong name given to something.

definition: To make less severe, serious, or painful; to lessen the harmful effects of something.
example: The defendant's remorse was cited as a factor that could mitigate the severity of the sentence.
mnemonic: MITI (gentle) + GATE — open a gentle gate to reduce severity.
confusableNote: militate: militate means to have a strong effect against something; mitigate means to lessen — easily confused.

definition: Restrained in style, size, or ambition; not showy or excessive.
example: The architect's modest design relied on clean lines rather than decorative flourishes.
mnemonic: MODEST = moderate + est — the least flashy option.

definition: Conforming to or following the current fashion; stylish and up-to-date.
example: The designer's modish collection drew acclaim from critics who praised its fresh, contemporary silhouettes.
mnemonic: MODE (fashion) + ISH: in the mode, modish — stylishly in vogue.

definition: To appease someone's anger or anxiety; to make less severe or intense.
example: The manager mollified the irate customer by offering a full refund and a formal apology.
mnemonic: MOLLI-fy: MOLLIS in Latin = soft — soften someone's hard feelings.

definition: Lasting only a very brief moment; extremely short-lived.
example: A momentary lapse in concentration cost the chess grandmaster the match.
mnemonic: MOMENT + ARY — lasting only a single moment.
confusableNote: momentary vs. momentous: momentary = very brief; momentous = of great importance.

definition: Tediously uniform and unvarying; lacking variation and therefore dull.
example: The lecturer's monotonous delivery caused several students to drift off during the long seminar.
mnemonic: MONO (one) + TONE: only one tone, never changing — boring by definition.

definition: Sharply sarcastic or biting in a darkly humorous or cutting way.
example: The novelist was known for his mordant observations about the pretensions of the upper class.
mnemonic: MORDANT = MORDERE (Latin: to bite) — words that bite deeply.

definition: Sullen, gloomy, and ill-tempered, with a tendency to brood silently.
example: After the argument, he became morose and refused to speak for the rest of the evening.
mnemonic: 'Morose' — think 'more gloom': someone who always wants more reasons to be gloomy.

definition: Having many different aspects, features, or sides; complex and many-dimensional.
example: The multifaceted crisis required coordinated responses from economists, public health officials, and diplomats.
mnemonic: MULTIFACETED: MULTI- + FACETS — like a gemstone with many cut faces shining in different directions.

definition: Lacking interest or excitement; concerned with the dull, ordinary aspects of everyday life.
example: After years of fieldwork, she struggled to readjust to the mundane routines of office life.
mnemonic: MUNDANE = MONDE (world): the everyday world — worldly and thoroughly ordinary.

definition: Remarkably generous in giving; characterized by lavish, noble liberality.
example: The munificent patron endowed the university with enough funds to build three new research centers.
mnemonic: Munificent: 'muni' (gift, like municipality giving services) + ficent (making) — making great gifts.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'magnificent' (splendid); 'munificent' specifically means extremely generous.

definition: Open rebellion by a group, especially soldiers or sailors, against their commanding authority.
example: The starving crew staged a mutiny, locking the captain below deck and seizing control of the ship.
mnemonic: MUTiny — people who won't stay MUTE defy their leaders loudly.

definition: Lacking foresight or intellectual breadth; focused narrowly on immediate concerns.
example: The committee's myopic focus on short-term profits ignored long-term environmental risks.
mnemonic: MY-OPIC: 'my optic' only sees what's right in front of MY face.
confusableNote: Literally means nearsighted (vision), but the GRE tests its figurative meaning of narrow-minded thinking.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: The lowest point of fortune, hope, or achievement; the point of greatest adversity.
example: Losing both his job and his home in the same week marked the nadir of his life.
mnemonic: NADIR sounds like 'no dear' — 'no, dear, it can't get any lower than this.'
confusableNote: Opposite of 'zenith' (the highest point); nadir is the absolute bottom.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Showing a lack of experience, judgment, or critical understanding; unsophisticated.
example: His naive assumption that all politicians act in the public interest was quickly shattered.
mnemonic: Naive sounds like 'nave' (church interior) — sheltered inside, unaware of the outside world.

definition: Excessive self-admiration and self-centeredness, often accompanied by a lack of empathy for others.
example: His narcissism was evident in the way he steered every conversation back to his own achievements.
mnemonic: NARCISSISM: Narcissus fell in love with his own reflection — total self-obsession.

definition: Just coming into existence and beginning to develop; emerging in early stages.
example: The nascent democracy faced enormous challenges in establishing stable institutions.
mnemonic: NASC-ent: from Latin nasci (to be born) — just being born, barely started.

definition: Failing to take proper care or fulfilling a duty; carelessly inattentive.
example: The negligent contractor left exposed wiring that later caused a fire.
mnemonic: NEGLIGENT = NEGLEcted + IGNore + ENT: one who neglects and ignores duties.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: A person who is new to a subject, skill, or belief; a beginner or novice.
example: As a neophyte investor, she made several costly mistakes before learning market fundamentals.
mnemonic: NEO-PHYTE: 'neo' (new) + 'phyte' (plant) — a newly planted seedling, just starting to grow.

definition: Causing irritation or annoyance; provoking agitation or discomfort.
example: The nettlesome bureaucratic delays frustrated everyone trying to file permits.
mnemonic: A 'nettle' plant stings your skin — nettlesome things sting your patience.

definition: To counteract the force or effect of something, rendering it harmless or ineffective.
example: The antidote neutralized the venom before it could cause permanent organ damage.
mnemonic: NEUTRAL + ize — make it neutral, stripping it of its power.

definition: Quick and light in movement or thought; agile and able to respond swiftly.
example: The nimble gymnast executed a flawless routine that left the judges breathless.
mnemonic: NIMBLE = sounds like THIMBLE on nimble fingers: quick and deft with hands and feet.

definition: Having or showing fine personal qualities such as courage, honesty, and generosity of spirit.
example: It was a noble gesture to return the wallet with all its contents intact.
mnemonic: Noble: 'no-ble' — no base motive, only high ideals.

definition: Existing in name only; so small as to be insignificant or merely token.
example: The fee was nominal — just one dollar to cover administrative paperwork.
mnemonic: NOMINAL = name-only: the title (name) exists but the substance does not.
confusableNote: In everyday speech 'nominal' can mean standard/normal, but the GRE sense means existing in name only or negligibly small.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Casually calm and relaxed; showing an easy unconcern or indifference about something.
example: Despite the high stakes, she answered the interviewer's questions in a nonchalant, unhurried manner.
mnemonic: French: 'non' + 'chaloir' (to care) — literally 'not caring,' cool and casual.

definition: So surprised and confused that one is unsure how to respond; bewildered.
example: The professor was nonplussed when the student challenged every point of the lecture.
mnemonic: non + plus — nothing more you can add; you're stuck at zero, speechless.
confusableNote: In informal American English nonplussed is sometimes misused to mean unfazed — the GRE meaning is bewildered, not calm.

definition: Harmful or poisonous to health or morals; physically or socially injurious.
example: Workers wore respirators to avoid inhaling the noxious fumes from the chemical plant.
mnemonic: NOX (night/harm) — noxious, like nox-ious gases lurking in the dark.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'obnoxious' (extremely unpleasant); noxious stresses actual harm or toxicity.

definition: To make legally void and of no effect; to cancel out or neutralize.
example: The appeals court decided to nullify the lower court's ruling on procedural grounds.
mnemonic: NULLIFY = make NULL — reduce to null, nothing, zero.

definition: Having a strong sense of the divine or supernatural; evoking awe and spiritual power.
example: Visitors often describe the ancient cathedral as a numinous space unlike any other.
mnemonic: Numinous comes from 'numen' (divine spirit) — filled with a god's presence.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with luminous (emitting light); numinous is spiritual, not literal light.

definition: Stubbornly refusing to change one's opinion or course of action; hardened against persuasion.
example: Despite the jury's sympathy, the obdurate judge refused to reduce the sentence.
mnemonic: OB- (against) + DURE (hard): hard against all persuasion — like stone.

definition: A gesture of respect or submission, such as a bow or curtsy; deferential homage.
example: The courtiers paid obeisance to the queen by kneeling as she entered the hall.
mnemonic: OBEI-sance: OBEY + -sance — the act of showing you obey through gesture.

definition: To make something deliberately unclear or difficult to understand; to confuse or bewilder.
example: The contract's dense legal jargon seemed designed to obfuscate the punishing fee structure.
mnemonic: OBFUSCate = ob + fuscus (Latin: dark) — to darken and cloud meaning.

definition: A harsh, sustained verbal reprimand or rebuke.
example: The board's objurgation of the CEO was unprecedented in the company's history.
mnemonic: OB-JUR-GATE: 'jur' (as in jury) + gate — hauled before a judgment gate and scolded harshly.

definition: Completely unaware of or inattentive to what is happening around one.
example: She was so absorbed in her book that she remained oblivious to the commotion outside.
mnemonic: OBLIVIOUS = OBLIVION: your mind has gone to oblivion, forgetting everything around you.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Not well known, discovered, or clearly expressed; hard to perceive or understand.
example: The professor's lecture drew on obscure medieval texts that few students had ever encountered.
mnemonic: OB (over) + SCURE (dark) — darkness cast over it, making it hard to see or find.

definition: Excessively eager to serve or please; fawning and servile.
example: The obsequious aide agreed with everything the senator said, never offering an independent view.
mnemonic: OBSEQUIOUS: OB + SEQU = following after — always following the boss around, kissing up.

definition: No longer in use or relevant; outdated and superseded by something newer.
example: The rise of digital streaming rendered the video rental store obsolete almost overnight.
mnemonic: OB-SOL-ete: 'solete' from Latin obsolescere — 'ob' (against) + solete (accustomed), no longer the norm.

definition: Stubbornly adhering to one's position and refusing to yield despite good arguments.
example: The obstinate mule of a professor would not accept late papers under any circumstances.
mnemonic: OBSTINATE = OBSTacle + -ate: someone who is themselves an obstacle to compromise.

definition: Noisily and stubbornly defiant; unruly and difficult to control.
example: The obstreperous protesters drowned out the speaker with chanting and drumming.
mnemonic: OB-STREP-erous: OB (against) + STREP (noise) — making noise AGAINST control.

definition: To remove a difficulty or need before it arises; to prevent by anticipatory action.
example: Installing better drainage systems would obviate the need for costly flood relief each year.
mnemonic: OB (in the way) + VIA (road) — clear the road of obstacles before you reach them.
confusableNote: obviate vs. mitigate: obviate removes the problem entirely; mitigate merely lessens it.

definition: To block or obstruct a passage, opening, or line of sight.
example: A blood clot occluded the artery, cutting off blood flow to the heart.
mnemonic: OC + CLUDE (close) — to close off completely, as in occluded artery.

definition: Involving supernatural, mystical, or magical powers and knowledge hidden from ordinary understanding.
example: The manuscript contained occult symbols that scholars struggled to decipher.
mnemonic: Occult = 'occult-ed' (hidden) — the curtain is pulled over secret knowledge.

definition: Extremely unpleasant or repulsive; inspiring strong dislike or hatred.
example: The committee found the terms of the agreement odious and refused to sign.
mnemonic: ODE + ious — an ode so bad it's disgusting; the opposite of praiseworthy.

definition: Asserting authority or offering unsolicited advice in an annoyingly intrusive, self-important manner.
example: The officious clerk insisted on inspecting every document twice, slowing the line to a crawl.
mnemonic: OFFICIOUS: too eager to be the 'official' — poking into everyone's business.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'official'; officious means overbearingly helpful, not holding an office.

definition: Giving the impression that something bad or unpleasant is about to happen.
example: An ominous silence fell over the crowd just before the verdict was read.
mnemonic: OMEN + OUS — full of bad omens; the omen is baked right into the word.

definition: To leave out or exclude something, either deliberately or through oversight.
example: The editor chose to omit the most inflammatory passages from the published version.
mnemonic: OMIT — O MIT: something MIssed ouT — left out entirely.

definition: Present everywhere or in all places at the same time; ubiquitous.
example: Social media has become omnipresent, shaping public discourse in nearly every corner of the world.
mnemonic: OMNIPRESENT: OMNI (all) + PRESENT — showing up everywhere, all the time.

definition: Having total, unlimited knowledge of all things.
example: The novel's omniscient narrator revealed every character's innermost thoughts.
mnemonic: OMNI (all) + SCIENT (knowing): an omniscient being knows everything everywhere.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'omnipotent' (all-powerful) or 'omnipresent' (everywhere at once).

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Involving an oppressive or overwhelming amount of effort or difficulty; burdensome.
example: The onerous reporting requirements consumed hours of the nonprofit's already limited time.
mnemonic: ONEROUS = ONE-erous: ONE task so heavy it feels like bearing the weight of everyone.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Having been something in the past but no longer so; former.
