example: The onetime champion now struggled to qualify for regional competitions.
mnemonic: ONE-TIME: it happened just once, in the past — not anymore.

definition: Not transparent in meaning; hard to understand or interpret.
example: The philosopher's opaque prose frustrated readers who could not extract a clear argument.
mnemonic: OPAQUE blocks light — just as frosted glass blocks sight, opaque writing blocks meaning.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'transparent,' its antonym, or 'translucent,' which allows partial light through.

definition: Exploiting circumstances for personal gain with little regard for principles or others.
example: The opportunistic investor swooped in to buy distressed properties the moment the market crashed.
mnemonic: OPPORTUNITY + istic — always hunting for the next opportunity to grab advantage.

definition: Harsh public condemnation and disgrace brought on by shameful conduct.
example: The company faced international opprobrium after the scandal was exposed.
mnemonic: OPPRESS + BRIUM: public oppression of your name — disgraced under the weight of public scorn.

definition: Elaborately decorated or adorned, often to a point of excess.
example: The ornate ceiling of the baroque chapel was covered with gilded reliefs and painted cherubs.
mnemonic: ORNATE = ORNAmented to the extreme — more decoration than needed.

definition: To become rigid, inflexible, or set in one's ways; to stop changing or developing.
example: Years of unchallenged authority had ossified the administration's thinking.
mnemonic: OSS = BONE: to ossify is to turn to bone — hard, fixed, immovable.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Conspicuously displaying wealth or luxury to impress others rather than for genuine pleasure.
example: His ostentatious collection of sports cars seemed designed to provoke envy rather than reflect genuine passion.
mnemonic: OSTENTATIOUS: 'ostent' means to show off — a lavish show put on for spectators.

definition: To drive out or expel someone from a position of power or place of belonging.
example: Board members voted to oust the CEO following the financial scandal.
mnemonic: OUST sounds like OUT — to push someone OUT of their position.

definition: Bizarre, strikingly unusual, or grotesquely strange in appearance or behavior.
example: The designer's outlandish hat — a meter-tall tower of fake fruit — drew gasps.
mnemonic: OUT-LAND-ish: behavior so foreign it seems to come from outside the known land.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To exceed, surpass, or leave far behind in growth, performance, or achievement.
example: Demand for electric vehicles has begun to outstrip the supply of charging infrastructure.
mnemonic: OUT + STRIP — strip past everyone else and go OUT ahead.

definition: Done openly and plainly, without concealment; not hidden or secret.
example: The administration's overt hostility toward the press was evident in its public statements.
mnemonic: OVERT — OVER in the open, no cover; the opposite of covert.
confusableNote: overt vs. covert: overt = open and visible; covert = hidden and secret.

definition: To quell the anger or agitation of someone; to bring peace to a conflict.
example: The negotiator worked tirelessly to pacify the hostage-taker and secure the captives' release.
mnemonic: PACIFY has PEACE inside (Latin pax) — make peace, calm the storm.

definition: Done with or employing great care and thoroughness; requiring diligent, laborious effort.
example: The restoration was a painstaking process that took conservators three years to complete.
mnemonic: PAINSTAKING = taking pains (great care) — every task done as if it costs effort.

definition: So obvious or intense as to be plainly felt or perceived; readily evident.
example: The tension in the negotiating room was palpable the moment the rival CEOs walked in.
mnemonic: PALP = to feel — palpable is something you can almost reach out and touch.

definition: Miserably small or inadequate in amount; contemptibly trivial.
example: Workers protested the paltry raise of two cents per hour as an insult given the company's record profits.
mnemonic: PALTRY: PALtry — pale and thin, so small it barely exists.

definition: A supposed remedy or solution for all difficulties or diseases.
example: Politicians often tout tax cuts as a panacea for all economic problems.
mnemonic: PAN (all) + ACEA (cure): from the Greek goddess Panacea who could heal everything.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Flamboyant confidence and stylish verve in manner or performance.
example: She accepted the award with such panache that the audience erupted into spontaneous applause.
mnemonic: PANACHE: a plume (panache in French) on a helmet — worn boldly to show off.

definition: Serving as a typical example or pattern of a class or category; exemplary of a model.
example: Darwin's finches are paradigmatic cases in the study of natural selection.
mnemonic: Paradigm + -atic — if something is paradigmatic, it IS the paradigm others follow.

definition: Seemingly self-contradictory yet possibly true; involving a statement that defies intuition.
example: It is paradoxical that the busiest people often seem to have the most free time.
mnemonic: PARA (beyond) + DOXA (belief): a paradox goes beyond ordinary belief — two truths that clash.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: A person or thing regarded as a perfect example of a particular quality or excellence.
example: She was considered a paragon of academic integrity throughout her long career.
mnemonic: Paragon: 'para' (beyond) + 'gon' (angle) — a shape beyond all others, the perfect model.

definition: Narrow in scope or outlook; overly focused on local concerns and indifferent to broader perspectives.
example: The politician's parochial concerns about his district prevented him from addressing national issues.
mnemonic: PARISH + -ial: thinking only about your PARISH — your tiny local area.
confusableNote: Parochial also means 'of a parish' (church-related) in a non-GRE context; on the GRE it signals narrow-mindedness.

definition: Excessively reluctant to spend money or use resources; extremely frugal to a fault.
example: His parsimonious nature led him to reuse tea bags and mend worn-out socks rather than buy new ones.
mnemonic: PARSIMONY — parse every penny; spend as little as possible.

definition: Constituting only a part; incomplete or not total.
example: The committee reached only a partial agreement, leaving several contentious issues unresolved.
mnemonic: PARTIAL: only PART of the whole — the rest is still missing.
confusableNote: Note 'partial' also means biased (favoring one side), so context determines which sense applies on the GRE.

definition: Adequate but not outstanding; good enough to be acceptable for a given purpose.
example: Her Italian was passable enough to order a meal and ask for directions.
mnemonic: PASSABLE: just enough to PASS — not great, but it passes the minimum bar.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Accepting what happens without active resistance or initiative; submissively inactive.
example: Her passive response to the injustice frustrated allies who wanted her to fight back.
mnemonic: PASSIVE = PASS on acting — you just let things happen to you.

definition: A work that imitates the style of another artist or era, often blending multiple sources.
example: The film was a pastiche of 1940s noir, complete with shadows, fedoras, and femmes fatales.
mnemonic: PASTE-iche: paste pieces of other works together into a new composition.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'parody' (which mocks the original) or 'plagiarism'; pastiche imitates admiringly.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Plainly and unmistakably evident; obvious.
example: His patent disregard for safety regulations put the entire crew at risk.
mnemonic: A PATENT is publicly registered — patent (adj) means openly, publicly obvious.
confusableNote: On the GRE, 'patent' as an adjective means obvious, NOT the legal document protecting inventions.

definition: To treat someone as inferior or childlike while appearing helpful; also, to be a regular customer of.
example: The senior partner tended to patronize junior associates, explaining obvious points as though they were novices.
mnemonic: A PATRON who acts like the big boss — treating others as beneath him.

definition: A minor fault, sin, or offense that is regarded as relatively harmless or trivial.
example: Forgetting to RSVP is a social peccadillo, not a serious breach of etiquette.
mnemonic: PECCA-DILLO: 'pecca' (sin in Latin) + small — an armadillo-sized sin, tiny and harmless.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Strange or unusual; different from what is expected or normal.
example: The researcher noted a peculiar pattern in the data that defied standard explanation.
mnemonic: Peculiar from pecus (cattle) — like an animal that strays from the herd; oddly different.

definition: Excessively concerned with minor details or rules, especially in academic matters.
example: His pedantic insistence on comma placement exhausted everyone in the writing workshop.
mnemonic: PEDANTIC = PED (teacher) + ANTIC: a teacher who acts antic over every tiny rule.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'erudite' (impressively learned); pedantic implies annoying obsession with trivia.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Lacking originality or excitement; dull and commonplace.
example: The critic found the novel pedestrian — competent but utterly without distinction or ambition.
mnemonic: PEDESTRIAN (on foot): nothing elevated — walking on the plain, flat ground of ordinariness.
confusableNote: pedestrian (adj) vs. pedestrian (n): as a noun it means a person on foot; on the GRE it means dull and unremarkable.

definition: Expressing contempt or disapproval; having a belittling or disparaging connotation.
example: The term was originally neutral but became pejorative after centuries of derogatory use.
mnemonic: Pejorative contains 'pejor' (Latin for worse) — a word that makes something sound worse than it is.

definition: Translucently clear in style or expression; easily understood.
example: Her pellucid writing style made the dense scientific research accessible to a general audience.
mnemonic: PELLUCid shares roots with TRANSLUCENT — pel + lucid — clear light shining through.

definition: A strong habitual inclination or liking for something.
example: He had a penchant for elaborate metaphors that sometimes obscured rather than illuminated his arguments.
mnemonic: penchant sounds like 'pendant' — a thing you wear close to your heart because you love it.

definition: Showing deep insight and the ability to understand complex issues quickly and accurately.
example: The professor's penetrating analysis exposed flaws that other scholars had entirely missed.
mnemonic: Penetrating = piercing through the surface to reach the hidden core of a problem.

definition: Engaged in deep, often melancholy thought; reflective with a tinge of sadness.
example: She sat by the window in a pensive mood, thinking of the friend she had lost.
mnemonic: PENSIVE: 'pens' write sad things — one who ponders tends toward sorrowful thoughts.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Extremely unwilling to spend; excessively tight-fisted and miserly.
example: The penurious landlord refused to repair leaking pipes, citing the cost as too great.
mnemonic: PENURY = extreme poverty/stinginess; penurious = acting as if always in penury.
confusableNote: penurious vs. penury — penurious is the adjective; penury (the noun) means extreme poverty.

definition: Lasting or recurring through many years; enduring and perpetual.
example: Budget shortfalls are a perennial problem for underfunded school districts.
mnemonic: PER (through) + ANNUAL — going through year after year without stopping.

definition: The quality of being deceitful and untrustworthy; deliberate betrayal of faith or trust.
example: The ambassador's perfidy was exposed when decoded cables revealed he had been feeding secrets to a rival nation.
mnemonic: PERFIDY = PER (thoroughly) + FIDE (faith in Latin) — thoroughly destroying faith.

definition: Carried out with minimal effort and little attention; done merely as routine duty.
example: The supervisor gave only a perfunctory glance at the report before signing it.
mnemonic: PER-FUNCTION-ory: done purely as a function — going through the motions without care.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Full of grave danger or risk; extremely hazardous.
example: The mountaineers faced a perilous ascent through whiteout conditions with visibility near zero.
mnemonic: PERILOUS has PERIL right in it — full of peril, dangerously risky.

definition: Of minor importance; relating to the edges or margins rather than the core.
example: The committee dismissed his concerns as peripheral to the main debate.
mnemonic: Peri- means 'around the edge' — peripheral issues circle the center but never reach it.

definition: Having a harmful effect, especially in a subtle or gradual way that is hard to detect.
example: The pernicious influence of misinformation eroded public trust in scientific institutions over years.
mnemonic: PER (through) + NICIOUS (harmful) — harm that works all the way through, slowly.

definition: To carry out or commit a harmful, illegal, or immoral act.
example: The investigation revealed that three executives had perpetrated the largest accounting fraud in the company's history.
mnemonic: PERPETRATE = PERP (perpetrator) + rate — the rate at which a perp commits crimes.
confusableNote: Perpetrate (commit a wrongdoing) vs. perpetuate (cause to continue indefinitely) — similar spelling, very different meanings.

definition: To cause something to continue or prevail indefinitely.
example: Stereotypes perpetuate inequality by shaping how people are judged before they speak.
mnemonic: PERPETU(AL) + ATE — making something perpetual by acting to keep it going.

definition: Continued, steady effort to achieve something despite difficulty or delay.
example: Her perseverance through seventeen rejections ultimately led to a landmark scientific discovery.
mnemonic: PERSEVERE = PER (through) + SEVERE (hardship): pushing through severe obstacles.

definition: Having a ready insight into things; shrewdly perceptive and discerning.
example: The perspicacious investor recognized the company's hidden value long before the market did.
mnemonic: PERSPICACIOUS = perspicere (Latin: to see clearly) — someone who sees through to the truth.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'perspicuous,' which means clearly expressed (of writing), not of a person's mind.

definition: Holding stubbornly and persistently to a purpose, belief, or course of action.
example: The pertinacious investigator pursued the cold case for a decade until it was solved.
mnemonic: PERTINACIOUS sounds like PERTINENT + TENACIOUS: tenaciously sticking to what's relevant.

definition: Relevant or applicable to a particular matter; having a direct bearing on the subject.
example: Please limit your comments to information that is pertinent to the agenda.
mnemonic: Pertinent = per- (thoroughly) + tenere (to hold) — holding thoroughly to the topic.

definition: Spreading through and present throughout every part of something.
example: A pervasive sense of distrust made cooperation between the departments impossible.
mnemonic: PER (through) + VASIVE (invade) — invading through every corner.

definition: Childishly sulky or bad-tempered; prone to peevish, unreasonable irritability.
example: The toddler's petulant refusal to eat anything green exasperated his parents.
mnemonic: 'Petulant' sounds like 'petu-lant' — a tiny pet (child) throwing a tantrum.

definition: A person who is hostile or indifferent to culture, art, and intellectual pursuits.
example: The billionaire philistine converted a historic art gallery into a parking garage without a second thought.
mnemonic: The biblical Philistines were enemies of the Israelites — a philistine is an enemy of art and culture.

definition: Having a calm, unemotional temperament; not easily excited or upset; stolid.
example: His phlegmatic nature made him ideal for crisis management, as he never panicked.
mnemonic: From 'phlegm,' one of the four humors linked to coolness — phlegmatic = coolheaded.

definition: Not genuine; fraudulent or insincere.
example: The art dealer was arrested for selling phony Picasso sketches to unsuspecting collectors.
mnemonic: PHONY — think of a PHONE call pretending to be someone you trust; fake all along.

definition: Visually charming or striking in a way that resembles a beautifully composed picture.
example: The picturesque village nestled in the Alps drew painters from across Europe.
mnemonic: Picture + esque: looks like a perfect picture.

definition: Devoutly religious, or making a hypocritical display of virtue and moral superiority.
example: His pious pronouncements about charity rang hollow given his well-known greed.
mnemonic: PIOUS — PI sounds like 'pie in the sky': lofty, self-congratulatory moral claims.
confusableNote: The GRE sense often implies hypocritical or ostentatious piety, not sincere devotion.

definition: Pleasantly stimulating or intriguing to the mind; agreeably lively and provocative in style.
example: Her piquant wit made every dinner conversation an event worth attending.
mnemonic: Piquant rhymes with 'keen aunt' — a keen, sharp-minded relative.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'pungent,' which describes an overpowering, often unpleasant smell.

definition: Concisely and forcefully expressive; brief yet full of substance and meaning.
example: Her pithy remarks always cut to the heart of the issue in a single sentence.
mnemonic: PITH is the core of a fruit — PITHy gets right to the core, no filler.

definition: To make someone less angry or hostile by conciliatory gestures or concessions.
example: The company issued a public apology to placate customers outraged by the data breach.
mnemonic: PLAC-ate: Latin placare = to calm — give someone a PLACE of calm.

definition: Not easily upset or excited; calm, peaceful, and undisturbed in manner or appearance.
example: The placid lake reflected the mountains perfectly in the still morning air.
mnemonic: 'Placid' — think 'place of peace'; a placid scene is a peaceful place.
confusableNote: placid vs. placate: placid = calm by nature; placate = to soothe someone who is upset.

definition: Expressing sorrow or mournful longing in a faint, wistful way.
example: The plaintive cry of the violin filled the concert hall with a sense of deep melancholy.
mnemonic: PLAINT-ive: a PLAINT is a lament — full of lamentation and sorrow.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'plaintiff' (the party in a lawsuit); plaintive means sorrowful in sound or tone.

definition: Capable of being shaped or molded; easily influenced or altered in form.
example: The brain remains plastic throughout childhood, readily forming new neural connections.
mnemonic: PLASTIC (adj) = like plastic material: formless until shaped by outside pressure.
confusableNote: plastic (GRE sense) means moldable/shapeable — not the synthetic material; the adjective predates the noun by centuries.

definition: A flat, overused remark or statement that has lost its meaning through excessive repetition.
example: The graduation speech was full of platitudes about following your dreams.
mnemonic: Platitude = flat (plat-) attitude — a flat, stale idea.

definition: Seeming reasonable or probable on the surface, though not necessarily true.
example: The alibi sounded plausible at first, but forensic evidence eventually dismantled it.
mnemonic: PLAUSIBLE = applause-worthy story — sounds convincing enough to earn applause, but may be false.
confusableNote: In the GRE deception cluster, plausible highlights something that seems true but may not be — contrast with 'specious,' which stresses the deceptive appearance more strongly.

definition: Easily bent or shaped; flexible and yielding without breaking.
example: The pliant willow branches bent gracefully under the weight of snow without snapping.
mnemonic: PLIANT = PLIABLE + ANT — ants bend around obstacles, never rigid.
confusableNote: pliable: nearly synonymous, but pliant more often implies willingness to yield; pliable emphasizes physical flexibility.

definition: Moving or progressing slowly and laboriously; tediously slow and unimaginative.
example: The plodding pace of the investigation frustrated reporters eager for results.
mnemonic: PLOD sounds heavy — imagine heavy boots thudding slowly through mud.

definition: A cunning plan or action designed to gain an advantage over an opponent.
example: Offering a free trial was merely a ploy to lock customers into a long-term subscription.
mnemonic: PLOY — think 'PLan of OYster' — crafty creatures that hide a pearl inside a trap.

definition: Having or showing determined courage in the face of difficulties; spiritedly brave.
example: The plucky underdog team beat the reigning champions with sheer heart and determination.
mnemonic: PLUCKY = PLUCK (guts/nerve) + -y: full of pluck, ready to face any challenge.

definition: Evoking a keen sense of sadness, tenderness, or regret, often with a bittersweet quality.
example: The soldier's letter home was poignant — full of love for his family and fear for his future.
mnemonic: 'Poignant' from Latin 'pungere' (to prick) — sharp emotions that prick the heart.

definition: Graceful self-assurance and composure, especially under pressure; calm dignity.
example: Her poise under the relentless questioning impressed even her critics.
mnemonic: 'Poise' — balanced like a scale (from Old French 'pois,' weight); perfectly balanced composure.

definition: To divide sharply into opposing factions or viewpoints with little middle ground.
example: The controversial legislation polarized the electorate, driving voters firmly to one extreme or the other.
mnemonic: Like the North and South POLES — total opposites, nothing in between.

definition: Strongly controversial or disputatious, typically in written argumentation.
example: The author's polemical essay sparked fierce debate among political scientists.
mnemonic: POLEMOS (Greek: war) — waging war with words and arguments.

definition: A person of wide knowledge or learning spanning many different fields of study.
example: Leonardo da Vinci, a true polymath, excelled in painting, anatomy, engineering, and mathematics.
mnemonic: Poly (many) + math (learning) = someone who has learned many things.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'polyglot,' which refers specifically to someone who speaks many languages.

definition: The quality of being excessively self-important or grand in speech and manner.
example: The critic's pomposity alienated younger writers who found his pronouncements insufferably self-congratulatory.
mnemonic: POMPOSITY: a pompous person acts like royalty in a pompous procession.

definition: To think carefully and at length about a question, problem, or weighty matter.
example: She sat by the window for hours, pondering whether to accept the prestigious but demanding position.
mnemonic: Ponder rhymes with 'wander' — your mind wanders through the problem slowly.

definition: Slow and laborious because of great weight or unwieldiness; dull and labored in style.
example: The professor's ponderous lecture style made even exciting topics feel exhausting.
mnemonic: PONDER-ous: so heavy it takes time to PONDER each slow, labored step.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To be a sign or warning of something, especially something momentous or calamitous.
example: Economists argued that the market volatility portended a severe recession.
mnemonic: PORT (carry forward) + END — carrying a sign of an ending or event ahead.
confusableNote: portend vs. portent: portend is the verb; portent is the noun (a sign or omen).

definition: Behavior adopted for effect or to impress others, with little sincerity behind it.
example: The politician's tough talk on crime was pure posturing ahead of the election, not genuine policy.
mnemonic: POSTURING: striking a pose — like a peacock spreading its feathers for show.

definition: Having great power, influence, or effect; capable of producing strong results.
example: The potent painkiller required a prescription and careful dosage monitoring.
mnemonic: POTENT = POTential ENTire: full potential entirely unleashed — maximally powerful.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Dangerously insecure or unstable; dependent on uncertain circumstances.
example: The fragile ceasefire left the region in a precarious peace that could collapse at any moment.
mnemonic: PRECARIOUS = PRE-CARE-ious — requires great care before acting, because the footing is shaky.

definition: To cause something, especially something undesirable, to happen suddenly or prematurely.
example: The assassination precipitated a chain of events that led to the outbreak of war.
mnemonic: PRE-CIPIT-ate: 'cipit' from Latin caput (head) — headlong rush, things fall forward fast.
confusableNote: precipitate (v) vs. precipitous: precipitate means to cause hastily; precipitous means extremely steep or rash.

definition: Dangerously steep or sudden; done with reckless haste and without deliberation.
example: The precipitous decline in stock prices wiped out billions in investor wealth overnight.
mnemonic: PRECIPICE + ous: standing at a precipice — steep, sudden, dangerous.
confusableNote: Often confused with 'precipitate' (adj., meaning hasty); precipitous focuses on steepness or dangerous speed.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To make something impossible in advance; to prevent from happening by prior action.
example: His prior criminal record precluded him from applying for the government position.
mnemonic: PRE (before) + CLUDE (close) — close the door before someone can enter.

definition: Showing abilities or maturity unusually early for one's age.
example: The precocious child was reading novels at age five.
mnemonic: PRE-COCIOUS: ripened (cooked) before its time — fruit that matures too early.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: A difficult, unpleasant, or embarrassing situation that is hard to escape.
example: Stranded without money or identification, she found herself in a serious predicament.
mnemonic: PREDICT + ament — a situation so bad you could have predicted the trouble.

definition: A pre-existing preference or special liking for something.
example: Her predilection for minimalist design shaped every product her studio released.
mnemonic: pre- (before) + dilection (love) — a love chosen before you even tried the alternatives.

definition: To be an early indicator or symbolic representation of something that will come later.
example: Many scholars argue that Hamlet's soliloquies prefigure modern psychological introspection.
mnemonic: PRE (before) + FIGURE — sketching out the figure of what comes before it arrives.

definition: To plan or think out an action, especially a crime, beforehand.
example: The prosecution argued that the defendant had premeditated the attack weeks in advance.
mnemonic: PRE (before) + MEDITATE — meditating on your plan before you carry it out.

definition: To be a sign or omen of a future event; to predict.
example: The sudden withdrawal of ambassadors presaged the outbreak of war.
mnemonic: PRE (before) + SAGE (wise prophet) — a wise sign coming before the event.
confusableNote: presage can be both a verb and a noun; as a noun it means an omen or warning sign.

definition: Having or showing accurate knowledge of events before they occur.
example: Her prescient warnings about the housing bubble went unheeded until the crash.
mnemonic: PRE (before) + SCIENT (knowing, as in science) — knowing things before others do.

definition: To authoritatively lay down a rule or course of action to be followed.
example: The constitution prescribes specific procedures for amending any of its articles.
mnemonic: PRE-SCRIBE: written down BEFORE — a rule written in advance for others to follow.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'proscribe' (to forbid or ban); prescribe means to order or authorize.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Overstepping proper bounds of behavior through excessive boldness or unwarranted assumption of privilege.
example: It was presumptuous of the intern to rewrite the senior partner's brief without being asked.
mnemonic: PRESUMPTUOUS: PRE-assumes too much — takes liberties before being given permission.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'presumptive'; presumptuous means overly bold, while presumptive means based on reasonable assumption.

definition: Claiming or implying importance, merit, or culture greater than is actually possessed.
example: The pretentious restaurant described its simple pasta dish in three paragraphs of florid prose.
mnemonic: PRETENTIOUS: full of PRETENSE — pretending to be more impressive than you are.

definition: To prove more powerful or widespread; to triumph over opposition or to be the dominant force.
example: Despite setbacks, the reformers' vision ultimately prevailed over decades of resistance.
mnemonic: PRE + VAIL (be strong) — to be strongest and gain the upper hand.

definition: To speak or act evasively so as to avoid the truth; to equivocate or lie.
example: When asked about the missing funds, the treasurer began to prevaricate, giving vague and contradictory answers.
mnemonic: PRE-VARI-CATE — vary the truth before telling it; to vary = to deviate from the straight path.

definition: Of the highest quality, importance, or value; being at the peak of excellence.
example: The athlete was in prime condition when he broke the world record.
mnemonic: Prime = first in Latin; prime quality = first-rate, the best.

definition: In its original, unspoiled condition; perfectly clean and free from any impurity.
example: The nature reserve's pristine forests had never been touched by logging or development.
mnemonic: PRISTINE = PRIST (pure priest) + INE: pure as an untouched altar cloth.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Strong moral uprightness; complete and confirmed integrity in character and action.
example: The judge's probity was legendary — no party had ever successfully bribed or intimidated her.
mnemonic: PROBITY shares the root PROBE — a person of probity can be thoroughly probed and found clean.

definition: A natural inclination or tendency toward a particular behavior, often a negative one.
example: His proclivity for risk-taking made him both a visionary entrepreneur and a financial liability.
mnemonic: pro- (forward) + clivity (slope) — you naturally slope forward toward that behavior.

definition: Recklessly extravagant; wastefully spending money or resources.
example: The prodigal heir burned through his inheritance in a single decade of lavish parties and luxury travel.
mnemonic: PRODIGAL — the Prodigal Son squandered his father's fortune on wild living.

definition: Remarkably great in extent, size, or degree; impressively extraordinary in scale or ability.
example: Mozart's prodigious musical talent was evident before he reached the age of five.
mnemonic: Prodigious = prodigy-ous — like a prodigy, astonishingly great.

definition: Competent and skilled in a particular area through practice and training.
example: After years of study, she became proficient in Mandarin and could conduct business negotiations fluently.
mnemonic: Pro-ficient: a professional is proficient — they've made their skill into a profession.

definition: Recklessly wasteful of money or resources, often combined with moral dissoluteness.
example: The profligate celebrity squandered millions on mansions and parties before declaring bankruptcy.
mnemonic: PRO + FLIGATE — 'flagrantly' wasting; profligately flagrant spending.

definition: Having great depth of insight, knowledge, or feeling; intellectually deep and penetrating.
example: Her profound understanding of human psychology shaped an entire generation of therapists.
mnemonic: Pro (forward) + found (bottom) — reaching all the way to the bottom, deeply insightful.

definition: Great depth of knowledge, thought, or feeling; an idea or statement of deep insight.
example: The philosopher's lecture was filled with a profundity that left the audience in reflective silence.
mnemonic: Profundity = the noun of profound — a vessel of deep meaning.

definition: Present in large amounts; produced or existing in great quantities; lavishly abundant.
example: She offered profuse apologies after inadvertently offending her colleague.
mnemonic: PRO-FUSE: 'fuse' from Latin fundere (to pour) — pouring forth in great quantity.

definition: To increase rapidly in number or amount; to spread and multiply abundantly.
example: Social media platforms have proliferated to the point of dominating daily communication.
mnemonic: PROLIF-erate: 'prolif' from Latin proles (offspring) + ferre (to bear) — bearing many offspring rapidly.

definition: Using too many words; tediously long and wordy in speech or writing.
example: The prolix report could have conveyed its findings in ten pages instead of eighty.
mnemonic: PRO-LIX: PRO (many) + LIX (words): prolific with excess words.

definition: To spread or transmit widely; to reproduce and disseminate ideas, organisms, or signals.
example: Misinformation propagates rapidly through social networks, often outpacing corrections.
mnemonic: PROPAG-ate: from Latin propagare (to extend by layering a plant) — making more and spreading out.

definition: A natural tendency or inclination to behave in a particular way.
example: Researchers noted a propensity among the subjects to overestimate their own abilities.
mnemonic: pro- (forward) + pens (hang) — like a pendulum that always hangs forward toward one side.

definition: Accurately predicting or foreshadowing future events.
example: In hindsight, the author's prophetic novel described the rise of surveillance states with eerie precision.
mnemonic: PROPHET + IC — having the quality of a prophet who sees the future.

definition: To win or regain the favor of a god, person, or force by appeasement or conciliation.
example: Ancient priests offered sacrifices to propitiate the gods before a military campaign.
mnemonic: PRO-PITIATE: pro (for) + pitiate (pity/favor) — act FOR gaining favor.

definition: Giving or indicating a good chance of success; presenting favorable conditions.
example: Spring brought propitious conditions for the harvest, with steady rains and mild temperatures.
mnemonic: PROPITIOUS = PRO + PITIOUS — everything is 'pro' (in favor), conditions are rooting for you.

definition: Conformity to conventionally accepted standards of behavior or morals; correctness of conduct.
example: Victorian society judged all public behavior by strict standards of propriety.
mnemonic: PROPRIETY = PROPER + -ty — the state or quality of being proper.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'property' (something owned); propriety is about proper conduct.

definition: Lacking imagination or originality; dull and uninteresting in an everyday, matter-of-fact way.
example: The critic dismissed the novel as prosaic, offering nothing beyond a plain recitation of events.
mnemonic: Prose (vs. poetry) is plain writing — prosaic means plain and dull.

definition: Occurring as if by divine care or intervention; fortunately timely in a way that seems foreseen.
example: Their providential escape from the collapsing building seemed almost miraculous.
mnemonic: Providence = divine guidance; providential = guided by a caring force.

definition: Narrow in perspective due to limited experience; unsophisticated and parochial.
example: Her provincial attitudes made her dismissive of any cuisine not from her home region.
mnemonic: PROVINCE + ial: stuck in the province — limited worldview, never left home.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Deliberately designed to cause anger, debate, or strong reaction.
example: The artist's provocative installation sparked furious debate in the press.
mnemonic: PROVOKE + ative: always provoking — poking others until they react.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Superior skill, expertise, or outstanding ability in a particular field or activity.
example: Her prowess on the chess board earned her an international grandmaster title at fifteen.
mnemonic: Prowess = 'pro' level — the skill of a true professional.

definition: A person or agent authorized to act on behalf of another; also, the authority itself.
example: Unable to attend the shareholders meeting, she voted by proxy, granting her attorney the right to decide.
mnemonic: PROXY sounds like 'pro-x' — X marks the one acting FOR you.
confusableNote: proxy vs. surrogate: both mean a substitute, but proxy typically refers to delegated authority in formal/legal contexts.

definition: Acting with careful good judgment and foresight to avoid risk or error.
example: It was prudent of her to diversify her investments before the market downturn.
mnemonic: PRUDENT = PRUDE + NT — a careful, cautious person who thinks before acting.

definition: Having or encouraging an excessive, unhealthy interest in sexual matters.
example: The tabloid catered to prurient curiosity with lurid celebrity gossip.
mnemonic: PRURIENT = PRURI (itch) + ENT: an itching, burning obsession with the indecent.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Childishly silly and trivial; showing a lack of maturity or seriousness.
example: The senator dismissed his opponent's puerile insults and redirected the debate to policy.
mnemonic: Puerile contains 'puer' (Latin for boy) — behavior fit only for a small boy.

definition: Eager or quick to argue, fight, or be aggressive.
example: The pugnacious debater interrupted opponents repeatedly and refused to concede any point.
mnemonic: PUGN (fist/fight) — pugnacious, like a pugilist (boxer) always ready to swing.

definition: Showing great attention to detail and correct behavior in conduct or etiquette.
example: A punctilious host, she ensured every guest was greeted by name and seated according to protocol.
mnemonic: PUNCTILIOUS = PUNCTUAL + meticulous — obsessively correct about every point (punctum).

definition: Practicing or imposing extremely strict moral or religious standards, often with harsh disapproval of pleasure.
example: Her puritanical upbringing left her unable to enjoy leisure activities without feeling guilty.
mnemonic: Think of the PURITANS — the historical sect famous for strict, joyless moral codes.

definition: Limited or restricted in some way; not absolute; also, having the necessary skills or credentials.
example: The committee gave the proposal qualified approval, endorsing the idea but insisting on several revisions.
mnemonic: QUALIFIED support = support with 'conditions' attached — not a full yes.
confusableNote: qualified (GRE trap): the GRE-tested sense is 'limited/conditional' (a qualified endorsement), not 'competent' — opposite of what students expect.

definition: An uneasy feeling of doubt, worry, or scruple, especially about one's own conduct.
example: She had no qualms about reporting the misconduct to the ethics board.
mnemonic: 'Qualm' — sounds like 'calm' with a Q for Question: a calm disturbed by an uneasy question.

definition: A state of perplexity or uncertainty about what course of action to take.
example: Offered two equally unappealing job offers, she was in a genuine quandary about which to accept.
mnemonic: QUAND (when?) — you can't decide when or how to act, stuck wondering.

definition: Enforced isolation imposed by authorities to prevent spread of disease or contamination.
example: Health officials placed the entire village under quarantine after confirming an outbreak of the virus.
mnemonic: QUARANTine = QUARANTA (Italian for 40) — ships once held 40 days before docking.

definition: To forcibly put down, suppress, or utterly defeat a rebellion, uprising, or idea.
example: The government moved quickly to quash the coup before it could gain popular support.
mnemonic: QUASH — squash it flat, like squashing a bug under your foot.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'quaff' (to drink deeply); quash means to crush or suppress.

definition: To suppress or put an end to a rebellion, feeling, or disturbance by force.
example: The governor called in the National Guard to quell the riots that had broken out across the city.
mnemonic: QUELL sounds like 'kill' — kill the chaos, silence it completely.

definition: Complaining in a petulant or whining manner; habitually finding fault.
example: The querulous patient constantly complained to the nurses about noise, temperature, and food.
mnemonic: 'Querulous' shares root with 'quarrel' — always looking to quarrel or complain.

definition: To argue or raise objections about trivial or minor points rather than the main issue.
example: Instead of addressing the core problem, she quibbled over the wording of a single clause.
mnemonic: QUIBBLE = QUIBBle: like quivering lips muttering tiny complaints, barely audible.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: In a state of inactivity or dormancy; temporarily quiet and still.
example: The volcano had been quiescent for two centuries before scientists detected renewed seismic activity.
mnemonic: QUIET + -escent = becoming quiet; quiescent things have gone quiet.

definition: Having or characterized by unusual, unexpected, or idiosyncratic traits.
example: Her quirky sense of humor made her lectures memorable even when the material was dry.
mnemonic: Quirky = full of quirks — unexpected twists that make something stand out oddly.

definition: Idealistic and visionary but impractically romantic and unrealistic.
example: His quixotic plan to end world hunger with a single app charmed but convinced no one.
mnemonic: From Don Quixote, the knight who charged windmills — grand dreams, hopeless odds.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Advocating or resulting in fundamental, sweeping change to existing systems or institutions.
example: The senator proposed radical electoral reforms that would completely overhaul campaign finance laws.
mnemonic: RADICAL comes from RADIX (root) — going all the way to the root to tear things up.

definition: Good-natured teasing or playful mockery; banter.
example: The colleagues' raillery during lunch kept the mood light despite the project's pressures.
mnemonic: RAIL: railroads rattle and tease your ears — playful rattling of someone with jokes.
confusableNote: raillery vs. ridicule: raillery is affectionate, good-humored teasing; ridicule aims to belittle or hurt.

definition: To speak or write at length without focus, wandering from topic to topic.
example: He tended to ramble during lectures, often forgetting his original point entirely.
mnemonic: RAMBLE: like a random RAMBLE through the woods — no clear path or direction.

definition: A consequence or outcome, especially one that is complex or unwelcome.
example: The CEO failed to foresee the legal ramifications of altering the safety data.
mnemonic: RAMI (branch) + FICATION: one decision branches into many downstream consequences.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Spreading or increasing unchecked and unrestrained; flourishing excessively.
example: Corruption was rampant in the agency, with bribes expected at every level.
mnemonic: RAM-pant: like a ram charging without restraint — wild and uncontrolled.

definition: Aggressively greedy; fiercely seizing wealth or resources at the expense of others.
example: Rapacious logging companies stripped the forest bare, ignoring environmental regulations.
mnemonic: RAPACIOUS — like a raptor; swoops down and snatches with predatory greed.

definition: Based on or in accordance with reason or logic; sensible and sound.
example: Despite the emotional circumstances, she remained rational and assessed the situation objectively.
mnemonic: RATIONAL shares RATIO — keeping things in proportion, balanced by reason.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'rationalize,' which means to justify poor reasoning; 'rational' itself is positive.

definition: To cause severe and widespread damage or destruction to something.
example: The wildfire ravaged thousands of acres of old-growth forest in a single week.
mnemonic: RAVAGE — savage + r, twice as savage, leaves only ruin behind.

definition: To formally withdraw or disavow a previously held statement or belief.
example: Under intense pressure, the scientist recanted his controversial findings.
mnemonic: RE-CANT: you CAN'T stand by what you said — you take it back.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'recant' and 'recuse' (to remove oneself from a case); recant is about withdrawing statements.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To summarize and restate the main points of something previously said or written.
example: At the end of the lecture, the professor recapitulated the three key arguments of the day.
mnemonic: RE-CAPIT-ulate: revisit the CAPITAL (main) points — go over the heads again.

definition: A person who lives a solitary life and tends to avoid other people.
example: After years in the public eye, the novelist became a recluse, rarely leaving her rural estate.
mnemonic: RE-CLUSE: RE + CLOSE — someone who closes themselves away from the world.

definition: To restore friendly relations between parties or make conflicting ideas compatible.
example: After months of dispute, the two partners finally reconciled their differences and resumed business.
mnemonic: RE + CONCILe — bring COUNCILs back together in peace.

definition: Not known by many; dealing with obscure subject matter that is difficult to comprehend.
example: The scholar's recondite knowledge of medieval alchemy fascinated but baffled her colleagues.
mnemonic: RECONDITE = re + condere (Latin: to hide away) — hidden knowledge, stored away from ordinary minds.

definition: A means of obtaining help or remedy when in difficulty; a resource turned to for assistance.
example: With all appeals exhausted, the defendant had no recourse but to accept the court's final ruling.
mnemonic: RE-COURSE — you return (re) to a course of action when stuck.
confusableNote: recourse vs. resource: recourse is specifically the act of turning to something for help; resource is a supply or asset.

definition: Breaking out again after a period of dormancy; experiencing renewed activity or virulence.
example: A recrudescent outbreak of the disease alarmed health officials who had declared it eradicated.
mnemonic: RE (again) + CRUDE (raw, unhealed) — a raw wound breaking open again.

definition: Formidably impressive and deserving great respect or fear; powerful and commanding.
example: The redoubtable general had never lost a battle in thirty years of military service.
mnemonic: Redoubtable: 're-doubt' — makes you doubt twice before challenging them.

definition: No longer needed or useful; saying or doing more than is necessary.
example: The editor cut several redundant paragraphs that repeated points already made earlier in the essay.
mnemonic: REDUNDANT = RE-DUN-DANT — done and done again; saying the same thing twice over.

definition: To improve something by making small adjustments; to make more polished or precise.
example: The engineer refined the prototype over six months until it met all performance standards.
mnemonic: RE-FINE: do it again (RE) until it is FINE — polished to a better state.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray
groups.subgroupLabel: Improve / fix / lower rank / go back

primaryGroup: Improve / fix / lower rank / go back

definition: To think deeply and carefully about something; to engage in serious contemplation.
example: After the failed experiment, the team took time to reflect on what had gone wrong and why.
mnemonic: A mirror reflects an image back; reflecting on thoughts turns them back inward for examination.

definition: To stop oneself from doing something; to hold back from an action voluntarily.
example: The judge instructed jurors to refrain from discussing the case outside the courtroom.
mnemonic: re- (back) + frain (bridle) — pull the reins back on your impulse.
confusableNote: refrain (n): also a repeated chorus of a song — a completely different meaning from the verb.

definition: To prove a statement or argument to be wrong or false by means of evidence or argument.
example: The scientist refuted the hypothesis with three independent experimental results.
mnemonic: REFUTE = RE + FUTE — think 'shoot it down again'; to shoot down a claim with proof.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'rebuke' (to criticize sharply) or 'repudiate' (to reject/disown); refute specifically means to disprove with evidence.

definition: To return to a former, less developed, or worse state.
example: Without continued therapy, patients often regress to their earlier harmful behaviors.
mnemonic: RE-GRESS: GRESS = step; to step backward (RE) to a previous state.
confusableNote: Opposite of 'progress'; regress means moving backward, not forward.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray
groups.subgroupLabel: Improve / fix / lower rank / go back

primaryGroup: Improve / fix / lower rank / go back

definition: To say or do something again, or repeatedly, for emphasis or clarity.
example: The instructor reiterated the safety rules before every lab session.
mnemonic: RE-ITERATE: ITERATE means to go through steps; RE-iterate = loop through them again.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To assign someone or something to a lower rank, position, or status.
example: After the merger, the veteran executive was relegated to a minor advisory role.
mnemonic: RE-LEGATE: a legate is an envoy; to relegate is to send someone away to a lesser post.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray
groups.subgroupLabel: Improve / fix / lower rank / go back

primaryGroup: Improve / fix / lower rank / go back

definition: To become less harsh or strict; to yield after firm resistance.
example: The stern judge eventually relented and reduced the sentence after hearing the defendant's circumstances.
mnemonic: RE-LENT: after being rigid, you LEND ground again.

definition: To voluntarily give up or surrender a claim, right, possession, or responsibility.
example: The retiring CEO agreed to relinquish all executive authority to her successor on the first of the month.
mnemonic: RE + LINQUISH — 'linquish' echoes 'relinquere' (Latin: to leave behind); you leave it behind for good.

definition: To take keen pleasure or delight in something; to enjoy with great enthusiasm.
example: The champion relished the challenge of facing a younger, faster opponent.
mnemonic: 'Relish' — like the condiment that makes food more enjoyable; relishing = adding zest to life.

definition: Worthy of attention and admiration because of being unusual or exceptional.
example: The restoration team did a remarkable job returning the cathedral to its original splendor.
mnemonic: Remarkable = worthy of being re-marked (noted) again and again.

definition: Intended to correct a deficiency or improve a faulty situation; corrective.
example: Students who struggled with algebra were enrolled in remedial math courses.
mnemonic: REMEDY + ial: relating to a REMEDY — fixing what is broken or lacking.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray
groups.subgroupLabel: Improve / fix / lower rank / go back

primaryGroup: Improve / fix / lower rank / go back

definition: Distant in manner or relationship; aloof and disengaged from others.
example: Despite living in the city, she remained remote from her neighbors, rarely acknowledging them.
mnemonic: REMOTE: like a far-off island, hard to reach emotionally.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with the everyday sense of 'geographically isolated'; here it emphasizes emotional or social distance.

definition: Payment or compensation received for work or services rendered.
example: The board approved a generous remuneration package including salary, bonuses, and stock options.
mnemonic: RE + MUNER — 'munus' (Latin: gift/payment) + ation; the repayment for labor.
confusableNote: remuneration vs. renumeration — 'renumeration' is a common misspelling; the correct word is remuneration.

definition: To cause something to be in a particular state; also, to provide or give something.
example: The hurricane rendered thousands of homes uninhabitable and rendered emergency aid impossible for days.
mnemonic: RENDER = to hand over or make happen — you render a verdict, render someone helpless.
confusableNote: render (GRE trap): students think only of artistic rendering, but GRE tests 'render + adj' (make) and 'render + n' (provide).

definition: To go back on a promise, agreement, or commitment previously made.
example: After signing the contract, the contractor reneged on his promise to complete the work by March.
mnemonic: RE + NEG: to 'negate' again what you already agreed to.
confusableNote: renegade: a renegade abandons a cause entirely; to renege is specifically to break a promise or deal.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray
groups.subgroupLabel: Improve / fix / lower rank / go back

primaryGroup: Improve / fix / lower rank / go back

definition: To formally declare one's abandonment of a claim, belief, or course of action.
example: She renounced her citizenship and relocated abroad to protest the authoritarian regime.
mnemonic: RE + NOUNCE — 'announce' your rejection; you publicly announce you're done with it.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'pronounce' (to declare or articulate); renounce means to formally give up or reject.

definition: An indirect, often unintended consequence that follows an action or event.
example: The new trade tariffs had repercussions felt by consumers and small businesses across the country.
mnemonic: Think 'percussion': you strike a drum, the vibrations ripple outward — repercussions are the ripple effects.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: The full stock of skills, works, or techniques that a person or group is prepared to perform or employ.
example: The violinist's repertoire spanned three centuries, from Baroque sonatas to contemporary concertos.
mnemonic: Re-PERT-oire: your PERformance STORE of ready skills.

definition: To restore to a full or sufficient supply; to fill up again what has been depleted.
example: After the drought, autumn rains finally replenished the depleted reservoir.
mnemonic: RE-PLEN-ish: 'plen' from Latin plenus (full) — make full again.

definition: To address someone with disapproval or criticism for a fault or offense.
example: She reproached her assistant for sharing confidential information without authorization.
mnemonic: RE + APPROACH — approach again to confront and criticize.

definition: A person of highly immoral character; someone given over to wickedness without remorse.
example: Neighbors regarded him as an unrepentant reprobate who had spent his life swindling the vulnerable.
mnemonic: REPROBATE = RE + PROBATE — someone rejected (re-) from moral probation; cast out as corrupt.

definition: To reject or deny the validity of something, or refuse to acknowledge a debt or obligation.
example: The senator repudiated the extremist statements attributed to him, calling them fabricated.
mnemonic: RE + PUDIATE — think 'repute' reversed; you destroy the reputation of something by rejecting it.

definition: Causing extreme disgust or aversion; morally offensive.
example: The jury found the defendant's actions repugnant and recommended the harshest sentence.
mnemonic: REPUGN: from 'pugn' (fight) — fighting against your stomach; it makes you recoil in disgust.

definition: To feel bitter indignation or ill will toward someone or something due to a perceived wrong.
example: He resented being passed over for promotion year after year despite his strong performance.
mnemonic: 'Re-' + 'sent' (feel again): to keep feeling the pain of an insult over and over.
confusableNote: resent vs. grievance: resent is the verb (the feeling); grievance is the noun (the cause).

definition: Remaining after the greater part has gone or been removed; pertaining to a leftover effect.
example: Years after the spill, residual toxins were still detectable in the river sediment.
mnemonic: RESID-ual: what 'resides' behind after everything else has left.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Able to recover quickly from setbacks, difficulties, or adversity; tough and adaptable.
example: The resilient entrepreneur rebuilt her company after bankruptcy and eventually thrived.
mnemonic: RESILIENT = RE- (back) + SILIRE (to spring): springs back like a rubber band.

definition: Admirably purposeful and determined; unwavering in pursuit of a goal or opinion.
example: The resolute activist refused to abandon her cause despite years of official opposition.
mnemonic: RESOLUTE = RE-SOLVED: the matter is firmly solved in one's mind — no rethinking.
confusableNote: resolute vs. resolve: resolute is the adjective; resolve is the noun (firm determination) or verb.

definition: Firm determination to do something; unwavering mental commitment to a course of action.
example: Facing immense hardship, his resolve to finish the marathon never wavered.
mnemonic: RESOLVE = RE + SOLVE: you've solved the question of whether to quit — you won't.
confusableNote: resolve (n) = determination; resolve (v) = to settle or find a solution — context distinguishes them.

definition: Able to find quick and clever solutions to difficulties using whatever is available.
example: The resourceful engineer repaired the broken satellite dish using components from a discarded radio.
mnemonic: Resourceful = full of resources — someone who always has a resource to draw on.

definition: Restlessly impatient or difficult to control, especially under restriction or delay.
example: The crowd grew restive as the speaker droned on past the allotted time.
mnemonic: Sounds like 'rest' but means the OPPOSITE — too agitated to rest.
confusableNote: restful: restful means calm and peaceful; restive means the opposite — uneasy and resistant.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: To repay an injury or wrong by inflicting a similar or comparable harm in return.
example: The nation threatened to retaliate with trade sanctions if the tariffs were not lifted.
mnemonic: RE + TALI (like talon/claw) — claw back in return for being clawed.

definition: Inclined to be silent or withhold information; not revealing one's thoughts readily.
example: The reticent witness answered questions in monosyllables and volunteered nothing beyond what was asked.
mnemonic: RETI-CENT: RETI = quiet (like retire); keeps feelings CENT-ered inside.
confusableNote: Often misused to mean 'reluctant'; reticent specifically means reluctant to speak or reveal information.

definition: Shy, reserved, and tending to avoid attention or social interaction.
example: Despite her brilliance, she was so retiring that few colleagues knew the depth of her expertise.
mnemonic: RETIRING: like someone who RETIRES to the back — quietly stepping away from the spotlight.
confusableNote: In this sense, retiring means shy — not the act of leaving a job.

definition: To reduce costs, expenditures, or staff; to cut back as a means of economizing.
example: Facing falling revenues, the company retrenched by laying off a quarter of its workforce.
mnemonic: RE-TRENCH: to dig back into your trench — retreat and reduce your exposure.

definition: An ecstatic expression of enthusiasm or delight; also, a freely structured emotional composition.
example: The food critic wrote a rhapsody about the meal, calling it a transcendent culinary experience.
mnemonic: RHAPSODY: WRAP-sody — wrap yourself in ecstatic feeling, like Bohemian Rhapsody's wild emotion.

definition: The art of effective and persuasive speaking or writing; sometimes, language that is impressive but empty.
example: The candidate's rhetoric stirred the crowd, even if the policies lacked substantive detail.
mnemonic: RHETO-RIC: a RHETORician uses rich language — the craft of speech.

definition: The quality of being extremely thorough, exhaustive, and demanding in standards or methods.
example: The scientific community praised the rigor of the decade-long longitudinal study.
mnemonic: RIGOR = rigid — rigor keeps standards rigid and unyielding.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'rigor mortis' (stiffness of death); in academic contexts, rigor means demanding thoroughness.

definition: Strong, vigorous, and full of vitality; representing excellence or peak condition.
example: The robust economy of the 1990s was the high point of post-war prosperity.
mnemonic: ROBUST = rooted like an OAK — strong, upright, at its peak.

definition: Involving or limited to the most basic, undeveloped, or elementary principles or skills.
example: The refugee had only a rudimentary knowledge of English when she arrived.
mnemonic: Rudiment = root; rudimentary = just the root-level basics.

definition: To think deeply and at length over something; to meditate or muse persistently.
example: He ruminated for weeks over the ethical implications of publishing his controversial findings.
mnemonic: Cows ruminate (chew cud) — they chew the same grass over and over, just as you chew the same thought.

definition: Regarded as too important or holy to be questioned, altered, or violated.
example: In the organization, the founding charter was treated as sacrosanct and beyond revision.
mnemonic: Sacro- (sacred) + sanct (holy) — doubly holy, absolutely untouchable.

definition: Deriving pleasure, especially sexual gratification, from inflicting pain on others.
example: The sadistic prison guard was finally prosecuted for the systematic abuse of inmates.
mnemonic: SADE — from the Marquis de Sade, whose writings defined cruelty as pleasure.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'masochistic' (deriving pleasure from one's own pain); sadistic involves pleasure from others' pain.

definition: Having or showing keen mental discernment and sound practical judgment; wise.
example: The sagacious elder foresaw the political crisis and urged the council to act preemptively.
mnemonic: SAGACIOUS = SAGE + acious — full of sage wisdom, like a wise elder.

definition: Promoting health and well-being; conducive to a healthy or pleasant environment.
example: Physicians recommended the salubrious mountain air as a remedy for her respiratory ailment.
mnemonic: SALUBRI-ous: from Latin salus (health) — full of health benefits.
confusableNote: salubrious vs. salutary: salubrious refers to physically healthy conditions; salutary means beneficial, often morally.

definition: Producing a beneficial or wholesome effect; promoting noble improvement.
example: The mentor's salutary influence helped the young student develop admirable discipline and character.
mnemonic: SALUTARY sounds like SALUTE — you salute something noble and good for you.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'salutatory,' which means serving as a greeting, as in a salutatorian's speech.

definition: Making a hypocritical show of religious devotion or moral superiority; self-righteously pious.
example: The sanctimonious politician lectured voters on family values while concealing his own scandals.
mnemonic: SANCTIMONIOUS = SANCTI (holy) + MONEY + ous — faking holiness for personal gain.

definition: Official approval or authorization; also, a penalty imposed to enforce compliance.
example: The treaty received the sanction of all member states, but nations that violated it faced severe economic sanctions.
mnemonic: SANCTION is its own opposite — it can bless or punish, a true GRE trap.
confusableNote: sanction: a classic contronym — 'gave her sanction' means approved; 'imposed sanctions' means punished.

definition: Optimistic, especially in a difficult situation; confidently hopeful about future outcomes.
example: Despite the setbacks, the team remained sanguine about their chances of meeting the deadline.
mnemonic: SANGUINE = SANG (past tense of sing) — you're so hopeful you feel like singing.
confusableNote: In medieval medicine sanguine referred to a blood-dominant temperament; on the GRE it means optimistic.

definition: Using irony, exaggeration, or ridicule to expose and critique human folly or vice.
example: The satirical novel skewered corporate greed by depicting executives as cartoon villains.
mnemonic: SATIRE + ical: satire = cutting wit to expose flaws — satirical is the adjective form.
confusableNote: satirical vs. sarcastic: satirical is a literary/artistic mode aimed at social critique; sarcastic is personal, often bitter mockery in speech.

definition: Barely sufficient in amount; meager or severely limited.
example: The expedition survived on scant rations for three weeks.
mnemonic: Scant sounds like 'scan't' — you can't scan much because there's so little.

definition: Severely and harshly critical; witheringly reproachful.
example: The drama critic delivered a scathing review, calling the production an embarrassment to the stage.
mnemonic: SCATHING: like 'scald' — burns the target with words.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: A formal split or division within an organization, especially a religious body or political movement.
example: The doctrinal disagreement caused a schism in the church, resulting in two rival congregations.
mnemonic: SCHISM — SCISSOR-like: it cuts a group cleanly in two.
confusableNote: schism vs. chasm: a chasm is a physical gap; a schism is a split within a group or institution.

definition: Brilliantly clever, lively, or dazzling in a way that sparks keen interest and admiration.
example: The professor delivered a scintillating lecture that kept students on the edge of their seats.
mnemonic: Scintilla = spark; scintillating = throwing off sparks of brilliance.

definition: Open contempt or disdain felt toward someone or something deemed unworthy.
example: The veteran journalist met the newcomer's overconfident claims with undisguised scorn.
mnemonic: SCORN: thorny and sharp — pierce someone with the thorns of contempt.

definition: Having or showing a strict regard for what is morally right; diligently careful and precise.
example: A scrupulous accountant, she documented every penny and never rounded figures in her favor.
mnemonic: SCRUPLE = a tiny unit of weight — scrupulous people weigh every moral decision carefully.

definition: Showing dedicated, careful, and persistent effort; diligently attentive to a task.
example: The sedulous copyeditor checked every line three times before approving the manuscript.
mnemonic: SEDULOUS = SED (sit) + -ulous: sits and works without stopping — devoted to the task.

definition: Allowing oneself excessive pleasure or gratification without restraint.
example: His self-indulgent lifestyle—spa days, fine dining, and shopping sprees—left him perpetually in debt.
mnemonic: SELF + INDULGE — surrendering entirely to every personal whim.

definition: Deliberately exaggerated or shocking to provoke public interest or excitement rather than inform.
example: The tabloid's sensational headlines distorted minor incidents into lurid scandals.
mnemonic: SENSATIONAL: manufactured to create a SENSATION — drama cranked to maximum.
confusableNote: Note the GRE sense (exaggerated/shocking) differs from the colloquial sense (wonderful/excellent).

definition: Excessively or self-indulgently emotional, especially about the past; prone to sentimentality.
example: The film was widely criticized for its sentimental ending, which many viewers found manipulative.
mnemonic: SENTIMENT + AL: ruled by feelings, often to an excessive degree.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Occurring by a fortunate and unexpected coincidence; pleasantly surprising by chance.
example: Her serendipitous discovery of an old letter in the attic led to uncovering her family's hidden history.
mnemonic: SERENDIPITY — coined from the fairy tale 'The Three Princes of Serendip,' who made lucky finds.

definition: Calm, peaceful, and untroubled; undisturbed by stress or agitation.
example: The monk's face was serene even as he described years of hardship and privation.
mnemonic: 'Serene' — like a serene lake with no ripples, glass-smooth and still.

definition: To cut off or divide sharply; to put an end to a connection or relationship completely.
example: The two nations severed diplomatic ties following the border incident.
mnemonic: SEVER: sounds like 'severe' — a sharp, decisive, complete cut.

definition: Something that is not genuinely what it appears to be; a fraud or imposture.
example: Critics called the peace agreement a sham because neither side had any intention of honoring its terms.
mnemonic: SHAM = SHAME without the 'e' — a shameful fake designed to fool.

definition: To avoid or neglect a duty or responsibility, especially out of laziness or cowardice.
example: A manager who shirks difficult conversations allows problems to fester unchecked.
mnemonic: shirk rhymes with 'work' — you shirk the work you're supposed to do.

definition: Having sharp powers of judgment; astute in practical matters, especially in business.
example: The shrewd negotiator spotted the loophole that saved the firm millions of dollars.
mnemonic: SHREWD rhymes with CLUED — a shrewd person is always clued in to what's really happening.

definition: High-pitched and piercing in sound; strident and unpleasantly sharp.
example: Her shrill whistle cut through the noise of the playground and immediately stopped the game.
mnemonic: SHRILL: SHRI-LL — SHRIEK with double L; a sound that SHRIEKS.

definition: A paid position requiring little or no actual work or responsibility.
example: The ambassador's nephew was given a sinecure at the embassy, drawing a full salary for minimal duties.
mnemonic: SINE CURE — Latin 'sine cura' = without care/responsibility; a job with no worry.

definition: Having many curves and turns; moving in a smooth, winding manner.
example: The sinuous river wound through the valley, doubling back on itself repeatedly.
mnemonic: SINUOUS contains SINUS — a sinus cavity is full of winding curves.

definition: To go around or avoid dealing with something directly, especially a difficult issue.
example: The report skirted the question of who authorized the illegal payments.
mnemonic: skirt = walk around the edge of something, like the hem of a skirt circles the body.

definition: Easily frightened or alarmed; nervously excitable and prone to sudden fearful reactions.
example: The skittish mare reared at the sudden crack of thunder.
mnemonic: 'Skittish' — like someone who skitters away at the slightest sound; quick, startled movement.

definition: Underhanded or unscrupulous behavior; trickery and scheming, especially in business or politics.
example: Investigators uncovered years of skullduggery in the bidding process for city construction contracts.
mnemonic: SKULLDUGGERY — skulls suggest danger and darkness; 'digging' up dirty schemes underground.

definition: The act of making false spoken statements that damage another person's reputation.
example: The politician threatened to sue the tabloid for slander after it broadcast fabricated allegations about his finances.
mnemonic: SLANDER sounds like 'slam' + 'her' — slamming someone's name with lies.
confusableNote: Libel is written defamation; slander is spoken defamation.

definition: An insult given by treating someone as unimportant; also, small or slender in degree.
example: She never forgot the slight of being overlooked for the award despite her superior performance.
mnemonic: SLIGHT — a light touch of disrespect; small in gesture but large in offense.
confusableNote: slight (GRE trap): as a noun/verb means to insult or snub; as an adjective means small — GRE tests both senses.

definition: Habitually untidy, careless, or dirty in personal appearance or work habits.
example: The inspector noted the slovenly state of the kitchen and ordered an immediate health review.
mnemonic: Slovenly sounds like 'sloven' — a sloppy, messy person.

definition: Slow-moving and lacking energy, vitality, or alertness; inactive.
example: The sluggish river barely seemed to move in the dry summer heat.
mnemonic: A slug moves very slowly — 'sluggish' describes anything that moves like a slug.

definition: Serious, earnest, and self-controlled in manner or thought; not swayed by emotion or excitement.
example: The committee took a sober look at the risks before approving the ambitious project.
mnemonic: 'Sober' — opposite of intoxicated by emotion; cool, level-headed, and clear-eyed.
confusableNote: sober (GRE sense) = calm and serious, NOT simply abstaining from alcohol.

definition: A dramatic device in which a character speaks thoughts aloud when alone, revealing inner feelings.
example: Hamlet's 'To be or not to be' is literature's most famous soliloquy, exposing his deepest despair.
mnemonic: SOL(o) + LOQU(y): SOLO + SPEAK — speaking alone to yourself.
confusableNote: Different from a monologue, which can be addressed to others; a soliloquy is always to oneself.

definition: Drowsy and inclined to sleep; inducing or characterized by sleepiness.
example: The somnolent audience could barely keep their eyes open through the droning lecture.
mnemonic: 'Somn-' is Latin for sleep (as in 'insomnia') — somnolent = full of sleep.
confusableNote: somnolent vs. soporific: somnolent = feeling sleepy; soporific = causing sleepiness in others.

definition: Highly developed in taste, culture, or knowledge; polished and refined in manner and awareness.
example: Her sophisticated palate could distinguish a fine Burgundy from a mere table wine.
mnemonic: Sophia (wisdom) + sophisticated: wisdom-seasoned, worldly-wise.

definition: Clever but deliberately deceptive or fallacious reasoning intended to mislead.
example: The politician's defense rested on sophistry, using technically accurate statistics to construct a deeply misleading argument.
mnemonic: SOPHISTs were ancient Greek teachers who argued any side for pay — sophistry is their slippery art.
confusableNote: sophistication: sophistication implies refinement and worldliness; sophistry specifically means deceptive, misleading argument.

groups.categoryTitle: Additional Source Terms by Semantic Tray

definition: Tending to induce sleep or drowsiness; so boring or monotonous as to cause sleepiness.
example: The professor's soporific monotone made even the most interested students nod off.
mnemonic: 'Sopor' is Latin for deep sleep — soporific things send you into a deep sleep.
confusableNote: soporific vs. somnolent: soporific = causes sleepiness (the agent); somnolent = feeling sleepy (the state).

definition: Involving ignoble actions; morally degraded and repulsive.
example: The tabloid gleefully published every sordid detail of the embezzler's double life.
mnemonic: SORDID sounds like 'soiled' — morally soiled and dirty beyond redemption.

definition: Reliable and based on valid reasoning; also, in good health or condition.
example: The auditors confirmed that the company's financial practices were sound and fully compliant.
mnemonic: SOUND reasoning stands firm — like a sound structure that doesn't crack under pressure.
confusableNote: On the GRE, 'sound' as an adjective means reliable or healthy — NOT noise; do not default to the everyday meaning.

definition: Thinly distributed or scattered; present in small or insufficient quantities.
example: Vegetation is sparse in the high-altitude regions of the Andes.
mnemonic: Sparse = 'space between' — lots of empty space, few things filling it.

definition: Showing or characterized by austerity, self-discipline, and the absence of comfort or luxury.
example: The athlete maintained a spartan lifestyle — no television, no alcohol, and lights out by nine.
mnemonic: Think of ancient SPARTA — its warriors lived without luxury to build discipline and strength.

definition: Superficially plausible but actually wrong or misleading; deceptively attractive.
example: The defense lawyer's specious argument impressed the jury but fell apart under cross-examination.
mnemonic: SPECIOUS = SPECIES of argument that looks good but is actually a fake — beautiful on the outside, rotten within.
confusableNote: Often confused with 'plausible' — specious emphasizes the deceptive, misleading quality, while plausible simply means believable.

definition: To form theories or conjectures without firm evidence; to engage in hypothetical reasoning.
example: Historians can only speculate about the true cause of the ancient civilization's sudden collapse.
mnemonic: Speculate = look through a 'speculum' (mirror) — you see only a reflection, not the real thing.

definition: A person who spends money recklessly and wastefully.
example: Known as a spendthrift, she had maxed out four credit cards before reaching thirty.
mnemonic: SPEND + THRIFT — the opposite of thrift; spending with no thriftiness at all.

definition: Occurring at irregular and infrequent intervals; scattered.
example: Sporadic gunfire was heard throughout the city but never sustained long enough to indicate a battle.
mnemonic: SPORADIC — like spores scattered randomly; no pattern, no regularity.

definition: Not genuine, authentic, or true; based on false reasoning or a mistaken claim.
example: The historian dismissed the document as spurious after carbon dating revealed it was a modern forgery.
mnemonic: SPURIOUS = SPURT of fakery — a spurious claim shoots out as if real but is counterfeit.

definition: To reject with contempt or disdain, especially a person's advances or an offered gift.
example: He spurned every offer of reconciliation, refusing to acknowledge that his former partner had any goodwill.
mnemonic: SPURN sounds like 'spur' — you kick something away with a spur, sending it off contemptuously.

definition: To waste money, time, or resources in a reckless or foolish manner.
example: He squandered his scholarship by skipping classes and failing to submit assignments.
mnemonic: SQUANDER — sounds like 'scatter and wander'; resources scattered and lost.

definition: Settled, sedate, and respectable in character; restrained to the point of dullness.
example: The staid accountancy firm frowned upon any unconventional dress or behavior among its partners.
mnemonic: STAID: stayed still — so settled in old ways that nothing ever changes.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'stayed' (past tense of stay); staid is an adjective meaning overly conservative.

definition: Loyal, reliable, and hardworking; strongly and steadfastly committed to a cause or person.
example: The stalwart supporters never abandoned the candidate even during her worst polling slumps.
mnemonic: STALWART = STALL (stand firm) + WART (rough toughness): stands firm, tough as can be.
